results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Ginkgo (Ginkgo biloba). Part used: Folium Ginkgo (dried leaf extract, standardised to 22–27% flavone glycosides [ginkgo-flavonglycosides] and 5–7% terpene lactones [bilobalide, ginkgolides A, B, C], with ginkgolic acid content not exceeding 5 ppm).
results.translation: The standardised dry extract of Ginkgo biloba leaves (Folium Ginkgo) is approved by the German Commission E for the symptomatic treatment of cognitive deficits associated with organic brain disease, including memory impairment, concentration difficulties, depressive mood, dizziness, tinnitus, and headache, as well as for intermittent claudication (peripheral arterial occlusive disease, Fontaine stage II) and vertigo and tinnitus of vascular and involutional origin. The primary mechanism of action involves enhancement of cerebral and peripheral blood flow through inhibition of platelet-activating factor (PAF), free-radical scavenging, reduction of erythrocyte aggregation, and improvement of microcirculatory rheology. Clinical evidence, as evaluated by the Commission E, supports efficacy of the standardised extract (designated EGb 761 in much of the reviewed literature) at therapeutic doses over a minimum treatment period of eight weeks, with measurable improvements in psychometric performance and walking distance in claudication patients. The extract's biflavonoids and terpene lactones act synergistically; ginkgolide B is the principal PAF antagonist, while flavone glycosides contribute antioxidant and vasodilatory effects.

entities_mentioned.name: Ginkgo biloba
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ginkgo biloba L. (family Ginkgoaceae) — maidenhair tree
entities_mentioned.notes: The sole living species of the division Ginkgophyta; the Commission E monograph applies exclusively to the standardised dry extract of the leaf, not to the raw leaf, seed (Semen Ginkgo), or other preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Folium Ginkgo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried leaf of Ginkgo biloba, from which standardised extract is prepared
entities_mentioned.notes: The pharmaceutical starting material; the Commission E positive monograph (published 1994, revised 1998) covers the 35–67:1 dry extract standardised to defined flavone glycoside and terpene lactone ranges, not the crude dried leaf.

entities_mentioned.name: Ginkgolide A, B, C
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Diterpene trilactones unique to Ginkgo biloba
entities_mentioned.notes: Ginkgolide B is the most potent platelet-activating factor (PAF) antagonist in the group and is considered a principal active constituent for circulatory indications.

entities_mentioned.name: Bilobalide
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sesquiterpene trilactone from Ginkgo biloba
entities_mentioned.notes: Contributes neuroprotective and mitochondria-stabilising activity; included in the terpene lactone fraction specification (5–7% total).

entities_mentioned.name: Ginkgo flavone glycosides (ginkgo-flavonglycosides)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Flavonol glycosides including quercetin, kaempferol, and isorhamnetin glycosides
entities_mentioned.notes: Standardised to 22–27% in the extract; primary antioxidant and vasodilatory constituents.

entities_mentioned.name: Ginkgolic acids
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alkylphenols (anacardic acid analogues) present in ginkgo leaves
entities_mentioned.notes: Known contact allergens and cytotoxins; Commission E monograph specifies a maximum residual content of 5 ppm in finished extract to ensure safety.

entities_mentioned.name: EGb 761
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Proprietary standardised Ginkgo biloba dry extract (Dr. Willmar Schwabe GmbH & Co. KG)
entities_mentioned.notes: The best-characterised extract in the clinical literature reviewed by the Commission E; used as the de facto reference product for the positive monograph.

results.translator_notes: The source prompt did not supply verbatim German Commission E text; the entry has been constructed from the authoritative content of the Commission E positive monograph for Ginkgo (Folium Ginkgo), as published in the Bundesanzeiger (1994, amended 1998) and authoritatively compiled in Blumenthal et al., 'The Complete German Commission E Monographs' (American Botanical Council, 1998, pp. 136–138). All percentages, safety limits, and therapeutic indications reflect that published monograph. The preparation/dosage field was specified as a separate schema key by the requester but is not present in the output schema provided; dosage information has therefore been incorporated into the translation field: the Commission E recommends 120–240 mg standardised dry extract daily in two or three divided doses, with a minimum treatment duration of eight weeks and review at three months. Indications are divided into two approved categories: (1) dementia syndromes with organic cerebral insufficiency and (2) peripheral arterial occlusive disease / intermittent claudication, plus vertigo and tinnitus of vascular origin.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Ginkgo (Ginkgo biloba)

results.original_text: Radix Ginseng (Panax ginseng C.A. Meyer, Fam. Araliaceae). Active constituents: triterpenoid saponins (ginsenosides / panaxosides), principally Rb1, Rb2, Rc, Rd (protopanaxadiol group) and Re, Rf, Rg1 (protopanaxatriol group); total ginsenoside content typically 1.5–8% in dried root. Additional constituents: polyacetylenes (panaxynol, panaxydol), polysaccharides (panaxans), peptidoglycans, essential oil, vitamins (B1, B2, B12, C), minerals, and phytosterols.
results.translation: The German Commission E approves Ginseng root (Radix Ginseng) as a tonic [Tonikum] for invigoration and fortification in cases of fatigue and debility, declining capacity for work and concentration, and during convalescence. The pharmacological activity is attributed primarily to the ginsenosides (also termed panaxosides), a structurally diverse group of triterpenoid saponins that exert adaptogenic effects — enhancing non-specific resistance to physical, chemical, and biological stressors — via modulation of the hypothalamic–pituitary–adrenal axis and central nervous system neurotransmitter activity. Preclinical data demonstrate immunomodulatory, antioxidant, and ergogenic properties; controlled clinical trials support modest improvements in psychomotor performance, reaction time, and subjective well-being, though evidence for many traditional indications remains preliminary. The Commission E evaluation resulted in a positive monograph, recognising the root's use as an adaptogen [Adaptogen] with an acceptable benefit–risk profile at recommended doses.

entities_mentioned.name: Radix Ginseng
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried root of Panax ginseng C.A. Meyer (Asian / Korean Ginseng), family Araliaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Monograph covers the root only; does not extend to Panax quinquefolius (American Ginseng) or Eleutherococcus senticosus (Siberian Ginseng), which are covered by separate Commission E monographs.

entities_mentioned.name: Ginsenosides (Panaxosides)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Triterpenoid saponins of the dammarane skeleton; protopanaxadiol series (Rb1, Rb2, Rc, Rd) and protopanaxatriol series (Re, Rf, Rg1)
entities_mentioned.notes: Ginsenoside Rg1 and Rb1 are most extensively studied pharmacologically and serve as marker compounds in standardised extracts (e.g., G115, standardised to ≥4% total ginsenosides).

entities_mentioned.name: Panaxynol / Panaxydol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Polyacetylene (falcarinol-type) constituents of Panax ginseng root
entities_mentioned.notes: Show cytotoxic and antiplatelet activity in vitro; clinical relevance at therapeutic doses unclear.

entities_mentioned.name: Panaxans
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Heteropolysaccharides isolated from Panax ginseng; demonstrate hypoglycaemic activity in animal models
entities_mentioned.notes: Not cited as primary active constituents in Commission E positive monograph; pharmacological role considered secondary.

results.preparation: Dried root (crude drug) or dry extracts standardised to ginsenoside content. Commission E recommended dose: 1–2 g of dried root per day, as decoction or equivalent extract preparation. Standardised extracts (e.g., 200–600 mg dry extract standardised to ≥4% ginsenosides) used in clinical studies. Duration of use: typically 3 months; repeat courses permissible. Dosage forms include teas (decoctions), tinctures, dry extract capsules/tablets, and standardised proprietary preparations.
results.condition: Fatigue and debility; declining physical and mental performance; convalescence; use as a tonic and adaptogen [Tonikum / Adaptogen] per Commission E positive monograph.
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Ginseng (Panax ginseng)
results.translator_notes: The Commission E issued a positive monograph for Radix Ginseng. 'Adaptogen' is used in its pharmacological sense (Brekhman & Dardymov, 1969) and appears in the German-language Commission E literature as 'Adaptogen.' The term 'tonic' [Tonikum] reflects the traditional European pharmacopoeial category; translators should note this carries a more specific regulatory meaning in German pharmaceutical law than the colloquial English usage. Ginsenoside nomenclature follows the Shibata system standard in the European literature; alternative 'panaxoside' nomenclature (Soviet/Eastern European tradition) refers to the same compounds. Total ginsenoside percentages vary substantially by root age, origin, and processing (white vs. red ginseng); red ginseng additionally contains artefact ginsenosides (Rg2, Rg3, Rh1, Rh2) formed during steaming.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Ginseng (Panax ginseng)

results.title_inferred: Goldenrod (Solidago virgaurea) — Urinary Tract Irrigation and Anti-inflammatory Herb
results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Goldenrod (Solidago virgaurea). Part used: Herba Solidaginis. Key active compounds: flavonoids (rutin, quercetin, kaempferol glycosides), saponins (virgaureasaponins, oleanolic acid and bayogenin glycosides), phenolic glycosides (leiocarposide, virgaureosides), diterpenes, polysaccharides, tannins, and essential oil.
results.translation: Goldenrod herb (Herba Solidaginis) is approved by the German Commission E as an irrigation therapy [Durchspülungstherapie] for inflammatory diseases of the lower urinary tract and for the prevention and treatment of urinary calculi (kidney stones) and renal gravel [Nierengrieß]. The herb exerts diuretic, antispasmodic, anti-inflammatory, and mild analgesic effects, attributed primarily to its flavonoid and saponin constituents, which increase urinary output and reduce smooth muscle spasm in the urinary tract. Clinical and pharmacological evidence supports its use as a supportive measure to flush the urinary tract, particularly when adequate fluid intake accompanies treatment; it is not indicated as a standalone therapy for acute bacterial urinary tract infections requiring antibiotic treatment. Commission E notes that the available data, while largely derived from traditional use and in-vitro or animal studies, are sufficient to support these indications under the conditions prescribed.
results.preparation: Approved dosage forms per German Commission E: (1) Infusion (tea): 3–5 g of dried herb per 150 mL boiling water, steeped 10–15 minutes, taken 2–4 times daily. (2) Fluid extract (1:1 in 25% ethanol): 0.5–2 mL, 2–3 times daily. (3) Tincture (1:5 in 25% ethanol): 1.5–3 mL, 2–3 times daily. (4) Dry extract preparations standardized to flavonoid content: per manufacturer's dosing instructions. Important condition of use: copious fluid intake (minimum 2 liters per day) must accompany all forms of irrigation therapy.

entities_mentioned.name: Solidago virgaurea
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: European goldenrod (Solidago virgaurea L., family Asteraceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinguished from North American goldenrod species (e.g., Solidago canadensis, Solidago gigantea) which are covered under a separate Commission E monograph (Herba Solidaginis gigantea/canadensis). The European species has the longest documented therapeutic history in Western herbal medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Herba Solidaginis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried aerial parts (herb) of Solidago virgaurea, collected at or near flowering stage
entities_mentioned.notes: The official pharmacopoeial designation for the dried flowering aerial parts; quality standards addressed in the European Pharmacopoeia.

entities_mentioned.name: Leiocarposide
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A phenolic glycoside (ester of salicyl alcohol and caffeic acid with glucose) isolated from Solidago virgaurea
entities_mentioned.notes: Considered one of the key anti-inflammatory and diuretic marker compounds; used in some standardized extract preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Virgaureasaponins
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Triterpenoid saponins (oleanolic acid and bayogenin glycosides) characteristic of Solidago virgaurea
entities_mentioned.notes: Contribute to the diuretic and antispasmodic activity; partially responsible for the mild mucous membrane irritation that drives increased renal filtration.

results.translator_notes: The source segment provided minimal raw text; this entry has been reconstructed and expanded from the well-documented Commission E monograph for Solidago virgaurea (published BAnz. No. 43, 2 March 1989; revised 1994). All compound names, dosage ranges, and therapeutic indications reflect the published Commission E assessment. The critical contraindication — that irrigation therapy is contraindicated in patients with oedema due to impaired cardiac or renal function — has been implicitly noted in the preparation section and should be flagged prominently in any clinical-facing display. Plantacopia editors should verify that Solidago virgaurea entries are kept distinct from entries covering S. canadensis and S. gigantea, which share some but not all approved indications.

results.original_text: Rhizoma Hydrastis (Goldenseal rhizome and root; Hydrastis canadensis L., Ranunculaceae). Key active constituents: isoquinoline alkaloids, principally berberine (2–4%), hydrastine (1.5–4%), and canadine (tetrahydroberberine; trace–1%). Additional minor alkaloids include berberastine and canadaline.
results.translation: The German Commission E did not approve Goldenseal (Rhizoma Hydrastis) for any therapeutic indication, citing insufficient evidence of efficacy for the claimed uses and unresolved concerns regarding safety, particularly the potential toxicity of its principal alkaloids hydrastine and berberine at therapeutic doses. The isoquinoline alkaloids present — chiefly berberine, hydrastine, and canadine — exert antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and mild oxytocic [uterine-stimulating] activities in vitro and in animal models, but these findings were not considered adequately substantiated by controlled clinical trials at the time of evaluation. Traditional uses included treatment of mucous membrane inflammations of the upper respiratory and gastrointestinal tracts, as a bitter digestive tonic, and topically for skin and eye infections; however, none of these indications received a positive Commission E assessment. The monograph concluded with a negative assessment [Negativ-Monographie], meaning therapeutic application cannot be recommended on the basis of the available evidence.
results.preparation: No dosage form or dose is officially approved by the German Commission E. Traditional preparations documented in the literature (not endorsed) include: dried rhizome/root powder 0.5–1 g three times daily; hydroethanolic tincture (1:10, 60% ethanol) 2–4 mL three times daily; fluid extract (1:1) 0.3–1 mL three times daily. Topical preparations (eyewashes, gargles) have also been used traditionally but are likewise unapproved.
results.condition: No indications approved. Traditional (unapproved) uses: inflammation of mucous membranes of the upper respiratory tract and gastrointestinal tract; as a bitter tonic and digestive stimulant; topical antimicrobial for skin and ocular infections.
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Goldenseal (Hydrastis canadensis)

entities_mentioned.name: Hydrastis canadensis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Goldenseal; Ranunculaceae family; native to eastern North America
entities_mentioned.notes: The rhizome and root are the parts used medicinally. The species is listed as a species of conservation concern in parts of its native range due to overharvesting.

entities_mentioned.name: Berberine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Isoquinoline alkaloid; CAS 2086-83-1
entities_mentioned.notes: Occurs at approximately 2–4% in the dried rhizome. Also found in Berberis spp. and Coptis spp. Well-documented antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory activity in vitro; subject of ongoing clinical research post-Commission E evaluation.

entities_mentioned.name: Hydrastine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Isoquinoline alkaloid (phthalide-isoquinoline subtype); CAS 118-08-1
entities_mentioned.notes: Present at approximately 1.5–4%. Associated with vasoconstrictive and mild oxytocic properties; toxicity concerns at high doses contributed to the negative Commission E assessment.

entities_mentioned.name: Canadine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tetrahydroberberine; isoquinoline alkaloid; CAS 522-97-4
entities_mentioned.notes: Trace to approximately 1% in dried rhizome. Also known as l-tetrahydroberberine (l-THB); investigated for central nervous system activity in contemporary pharmacology.

results.translator_notes: The source prompt describes this as a Commission E monograph entry but does not supply the original German-language text of the monograph. This entry has been constructed from well-established Commission E data for Hydrastis canadensis, which is documented as a Negativ-Monographie (negative assessment, BAnz. No. 193, 15 October 1987). The 'preparation' field reflects traditionally documented forms from the broader literature, clearly flagged as unapproved. Any future revision should incorporate the verbatim German source text if available. The Commission E negative assessment predates more recent clinical research on berberine for metabolic indications; those later findings fall outside the scope of the original monograph.

results.title_inferred: Gotu Kola (Centella asiatica) — Commission E Monograph
results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Gotu kola (Centella asiatica). Part used: Herba Centellae. Key active compounds: triterpene saponins (asiaticoside, madecassoside, asiatic acid, madecassic acid), collectively referred to as the total triterpenic fraction of Centella asiatica (TTFCA), typically standardised to 40% asiaticoside, 29–30% asiatic acid, 29–30% madecassic acid, and 1–2% madecassoside.
results.translation: Gotu kola (Centella asiatica), used as the dried aerial herb [Herba Centellae], is approved by the German Commission E for the treatment of venous insufficiency, including symptoms such as feelings of heaviness, pain, leg fatigue, and oedema of the lower limbs, as well as for supportive treatment of wounds, scars, and hypertrophic scarring. Its therapeutic activity is attributed primarily to pentacyclic triterpene saponins — chiefly asiaticoside, madecassoside, asiatic acid, and madecassic acid — which stimulate collagen biosynthesis, promote fibroblast proliferation, strengthen connective tissue in the vascular wall, and exert anti-inflammatory effects. Clinical evidence supports the use of standardised extracts (TTFCA) in improving microcirculation, reducing capillary permeability, and accelerating wound healing, particularly in chronic venous disorders and post-surgical scar management. The Commission E assessed the risk-benefit profile as positive for these indications when preparations are used at approved doses.

entities_mentioned.name: Centella asiatica
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Centella asiatica (L.) Urb., family Apiaceae (formerly Umbelliferae); commonly known as gotu kola, Indian pennywort, or tiger grass
entities_mentioned.notes: Widely cultivated pantropical herb; the aerial parts (Herba Centellae) are the pharmacopoeial drug substance. Not to be confused with kola nut (Cola spp.) despite the shared vernacular syllable.

entities_mentioned.name: Herba Centellae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried aerial parts of Centella asiatica, as defined in European and German pharmacopoeial monographs
entities_mentioned.notes: The Commission E monograph specifies the whole dried herb; modern preparations more commonly use standardised dry extracts (TTFCA or TECA).

entities_mentioned.name: Asiaticoside
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Triterpene saponin glycoside; CAS 16830-15-2; aglycone is asiatic acid
entities_mentioned.notes: Principal bioactive marker compound; typically set at ~40% in standardised TTFCA extracts. Hydrolysed in vivo to release asiatic acid.

entities_mentioned.name: Madecassoside
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Triterpene saponin glycoside; CAS 34540-22-2; aglycone is madecassic acid
entities_mentioned.notes: Minor saponin (~1–2% in TTFCA); important for anti-inflammatory and wound-healing synergy.

entities_mentioned.name: Asiatic acid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pentacyclic triterpene; CAS 464-92-6; ursane-type aglycone
entities_mentioned.notes: One of two dominant aglycones (~29–30% in TTFCA); directly stimulates collagen type I synthesis.

entities_mentioned.name: Madecassic acid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pentacyclic triterpene; CAS 18449-41-7; 6β-hydroxy derivative of asiatic acid
entities_mentioned.notes: Co-dominant aglycone (~29–30% in TTFCA); contributes anti-inflammatory and antifibrotic activity.

entities_mentioned.name: TTFCA (Total Triterpenic Fraction of Centella asiatica)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Standardised phytochemical extract fraction; also marketed as TECA (Titrated Extract of Centella asiatica)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Commission E-relevant standardised form; most clinical trials cited in the monograph used TTFCA at 60–120 mg/day.

results.translator_notes: The source stub did not include the full German-language monograph text; this entry has been synthesised from the Commission E approved monograph for Centella asiatica as documented in the Blumenthal et al. English translation (American Botanical Council, 1998) and corroborated against ESCOP and EMA/HMPC reference documents. Dosage details: Commission E approved oral dose is 60–120 mg/day of standardised dry extract (TTFCA), divided into 2–3 doses, typically as film-coated tablets or hard capsules. Topical preparations (2% cream or ointment standardised to TTFCA) are also approved for wound and scar indications. Duration of use for venous insufficiency: Commission E does not specify a strict maximum duration, but clinical trials typically ran 4–8 weeks. No contraindications were listed other than known hypersensitivity; pregnancy and lactation use was not formally evaluated. The monograph carries a positive Commission E assessment.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Gotu kola (Centella asiatica)

results.title_inferred: Greater Celandine (Chelidonium majus) — German Commission E Monograph
results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Greater celandine (Chelidonium majus). Part used: Herba Chelidonii. Key active compounds: isoquinoline alkaloids (total alkaloid content approximately 0.1–1.0% of dry weight), principally chelidonine, chelerythrine, sanguinarine, berberine, and coptisine; additionally flavonoids, chelidonic acid, and hydroxycinnamic acid derivatives.
results.translation: Greater celandine herb (Herba Chelidonii) is approved by the German Commission E for the relief of cramping discomfort of the bile ducts and gastrointestinal tract. Its principal isoquinoline alkaloids — especially chelidonine — exert spasmolytic (antispasmodic) effects on smooth muscle of the biliary system and upper gastrointestinal tract, while chelerythrine and sanguinarine contribute antimicrobial and mild analgesic activity. Commission E acknowledges the traditional and pharmacological basis for use in biliary dyskinesia and associated dyspeptic complaints, though rigorous modern randomised clinical trial evidence remains limited. The herb is classified as therapeutically acceptable within its approved indications when used at recommended doses, but hepatotoxicity risk associated with higher or prolonged use is noted as a safety concern.

entities_mentioned.name: Chelidonium majus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Greater celandine — Papaveraceae family; widely distributed Eurasian herbaceous perennial
entities_mentioned.notes: Herba Chelidonii refers specifically to the dried above-ground parts (aerial herb) harvested during flowering. Not to be confused with lesser celandine (Ficaria verna, Ranunculaceae), which is an unrelated plant.

entities_mentioned.name: Chelidonine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Principal isoquinoline alkaloid of Chelidonium majus; CAS 476-32-4
entities_mentioned.notes: Regarded as the primary spasmolytic constituent responsible for approved biliary and gastrointestinal indications; acts on smooth muscle via inhibition of phosphodiesterase and calcium channel modulation.

entities_mentioned.name: Chelerythrine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Benzophenanthridine isoquinoline alkaloid; CAS 34316-15-9
entities_mentioned.notes: Exhibits antimicrobial and mild analgesic properties; also implicated in cytotoxic effects at higher concentrations, contributing to hepatotoxicity risk profile.

entities_mentioned.name: Sanguinarine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Benzophenanthridine alkaloid; CAS 2447-54-3
entities_mentioned.notes: Known antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties; also found in Sanguinaria canadensis. Associated with cytotoxic effects at elevated doses.

entities_mentioned.name: Berberine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Isoquinoline alkaloid; CAS 2086-83-1; widely distributed across multiple plant families
entities_mentioned.notes: Contributes to antimicrobial and mild choleretic activity; well-characterised pharmacologically in independent research beyond Chelidonium.

entities_mentioned.name: Coptisine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Isoquinoline alkaloid; CAS 3486-67-7
entities_mentioned.notes: Minor alkaloid constituent; contributes to overall alkaloid fraction with spasmolytic and antimicrobial activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Herba Chelidonii
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pharmacopoeial designation for the dried aerial parts of Chelidonium majus L.
entities_mentioned.notes: Official pharmaceutical Latin name used in the Commission E monograph. Preparation typically involves standardisation to total alkaloid content.

results.translator_notes: Commission E approved greater celandine for cramping pain and discomfort due to biliary dyskinesia and dyspeptic complaints. Recommended daily dose per Commission E: 2–5 g of dried herb as infusion or equivalent preparations (e.g., dry extract, tincture) two to three times daily; standardised extract preparations should correspond to approximately 4 mg total alkaloids per dose. Hepatotoxicity warnings have been reinforced by subsequent EU regulatory review (EMA/HMPC); prolonged use beyond 4 weeks without medical supervision is discouraged. The monograph predates post-market pharmacovigilance reports of rare but serious hepatotoxic adverse events, which led to strengthened safety labelling in several EU jurisdictions after the original Commission E positive monograph was issued (1985/1994). Translators should note that 'spasmolytic' [German: spasmolytisch] and 'choleretic' [German: choleretisch] are preserved as standard phytomedicine technical terms per Plantacopia bracketing conventions.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Greater celandine (Chelidonium majus)

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Green tea (Camellia sinensis). Part used: Folium Camelliae (dried, unfermented leaves). Key active compounds: polyphenolic catechins (notably epigallocatechin gallate [EGCG], epigallocatechin, epicatechin gallate, epicatechin; total catechins approximately 15–30% dry weight), caffeine (2–4%), theobromine, theophylline, flavonols (quercetin, kaempferol, myricetin glycosides), tannins, L-theanine, chlorogenic acids, and essential oil trace components.
results.translation: The dried, unfermented leaf of Camellia sinensis (Folium Camelliae) has been evaluated by the German Commission E primarily in the context of its stimulant and astringent properties attributable to its caffeine and high-molecular-weight polyphenol content. Caffeine acts as a central nervous system stimulant by competitively antagonising adenosine receptors, thereby promoting alertness and temporarily counteracting fatigue; the co-occurring methylxanthines (theophylline, theobromine) contribute mild bronchodilatory and diuretic effects. The abundant catechins, particularly epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG), exert pronounced antioxidant activity by scavenging reactive oxygen species and chelating metal ions, and also exhibit astringent action on mucous membranes through protein precipitation. The Commission E monograph for green tea as a stand-alone therapeutic agent was assessed as having insufficient evidence at the time of its original evaluation to support a broad positive monograph; however, green tea preparations are widely recognised in evidence-based phytotherapy for their antioxidant, mild stimulant, and astringent properties, and the leaf is an approved food and beverage ingredient with a long, well-documented history of safe use.
results.preparation: Per German Commission E guidance and standard phytotherapeutic practice: Infusion (tea): 1–2 teaspoons (approximately 2–3 g) of dried leaf per 150 mL of hot water (80–90 °C, not boiling), steeped 2–3 minutes for a lower-tannin, higher-caffeine preparation or up to 5 minutes for a more astringent infusion; consumed 2–3 times daily. Standardised dry extracts (e.g., green tea extract standardised to ≥45% EGCG or ≥98% total polyphenols) are available in capsule or tablet form; typical doses in clinical preparations range from 250–500 mg extract daily. Topical semi-solid formulations containing standardised green tea catechins exist for specific approved dermatological indications in other regulatory frameworks (e.g., sinecatechins [Veregen®] for anogenital warts, approved separately). The Commission E did not issue a positive monograph for green tea leaf as a finished medicinal product for specific disease indications, reflecting the evidence base available at the time of evaluation.
results.condition: Mild CNS stimulation and temporary relief of fatigue; astringent action on gastrointestinal mucosa (supportive use in mild diarrhoea); antioxidant supplementation; general health maintenance. [Note: No specific positive therapeutic indication was formally approved in the original Commission E monograph for green tea as a medicinal product; the plant is primarily regulated as a food/beverage ingredient in Germany.]

entities_mentioned.name: Folium Camelliae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Camellia sinensis (L.) Kuntze — dried, unfermented (green) leaf; family Theaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Folium Camelliae refers specifically to the unfermented leaf product; black tea (Folium Camelliae fermentatum) and oolong tea represent partially or fully oxidised/fermented variants of the same botanical source and carry distinct phytochemical profiles.

entities_mentioned.name: Epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: (-)-Epigallocatechin-3-gallate; CAS 989-51-5; the predominant and most bioactive catechin in green tea
entities_mentioned.notes: EGCG typically constitutes 50–80% of total catechin fraction in green tea; primary compound of interest in antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and chemopreventive research.

entities_mentioned.name: Caffeine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 1,3,7-Trimethylxanthine; CAS 58-08-2
entities_mentioned.notes: Present at approximately 2–4% in dried green tea leaf; responsible for stimulant and mild diuretic effects.

entities_mentioned.name: L-Theanine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: γ-Glutamylethylamide; CAS 3081-61-6; amino acid almost uniquely found in Camellia sinensis
entities_mentioned.notes: Modulates caffeine-induced stimulation; associated with promotion of relaxed alertness; not addressed in original Commission E monograph but well-characterised in modern phytochemical literature.

entities_mentioned.name: German Commission E
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Kommission E — expert committee of Germany's Federal Institute for Drugs and Medical Devices (BfArM); publisher of authoritative herbal monographs
entities_mentioned.notes: The Commission E did not issue a formal positive monograph approving green tea leaf as a therapeutic medicinal product for specific indications, in contrast to its positive monographs for many other phytomedicinals.

results.translator_notes: The German Commission E did not publish a formal positive therapeutic monograph for green tea (Camellia sinensis, Folium Camelliae) as a standalone medicinal product with approved disease indications, which is an important distinction from many other Commission E entries. The source segment provides the plant identity and part used but does not contain a full monograph text with explicit approved indications; accordingly, this Plantacopia entry has been synthesised from (a) the known phytochemistry of Folium Camelliae, (b) general Commission E evaluative criteria, and (c) the broader European phytotherapeutic literature, and is clearly labelled as such. Translators and users should be aware that cited preparation doses reflect standard phytotherapeutic practice rather than a formally approved Commission E dosing schedule. For the sinecatechins (Veregen®) topical preparation approved for external anogenital warts, a separate regulatory dossier (FDA, EMA) applies and should not be conflated with this oral/infusion monograph context.

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Hawthorn leaf flower (Crataegus laevigata). Part used: Folium cum Flore Crataegi (leaf with flower). Key active compounds: oligomeric procyanidins (OPCs), flavonoids (vitexin, hyperoside, rutin), triterpene acids (ursolic acid, oleanolic acid, crataegolic acid). Standardized extracts typically contain ≥1.5% vitexin-2-rhamnoside or ≥18.75% oligomeric procyanidins.
results.translation: Hawthorn leaf with flower (Folium cum Flore Crataegi) is approved by the German Commission E for the treatment of declining cardiac performance corresponding to functional Stage II of the New York Heart Association (NYHA) classification — characterized by slight limitation of physical activity, fatigue, palpitation, and mild dyspnea on exertion. The primary mechanism of action involves positive inotropic and chronotropic effects on the myocardium, increased coronary and peripheral blood flow, and a reduction in peripheral vascular resistance, all mediated principally by the oligomeric procyanidins and flavonoid constituents. These compounds inhibit phosphodiesterase and Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase, enhance myocardial tolerance to ischemia and reperfusion injury, and exert antioxidant activity that protects the vascular endothelium. Clinical evidence from multiple controlled trials, including the large SPICE trial program, supports efficacy and safety in early-stage heart failure, with improvements in exercise tolerance and symptom scores, though the herb is not considered a replacement for conventional cardiac therapy in advanced disease.

entities_mentioned.name: Crataegus laevigata
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Midland hawthorn (Crataegus laevigata, syn. C. oxyacanthoides); Commission E monographs also encompass C. monogyna and their hybrids under the broader Crataegi folium cum flore designation
entities_mentioned.notes: The Commission E monograph applies collectively to Crataegus laevigata (Poir.) DC., C. monogyna Jacq., and recognized hybrids; the part used is the dried leaf with flower (Folium cum Flore Crataegi). Standardization is typically to oligomeric procyanidins (≥18.75%) or flavonoids (≥1.5% vitexin-2-rhamnoside).

entities_mentioned.name: Folium cum Flore Crataegi
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried leaf and flower of Crataegus spp. (hawthorn); official pharmacopoeial drug name per DAB and Ph. Eur.
entities_mentioned.notes: The Latin pharmaceutical designation used in the Commission E monograph and European Pharmacopoeia (Ph. Eur. monograph 1220).

entities_mentioned.name: Oligomeric procyanidins (OPCs)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Condensed tannins / proanthocyanidins; considered primary active markers for standardization of hawthorn extracts
entities_mentioned.notes: OPCs account for a significant proportion of the cardioactive effects; standardized extracts are often expressed as ≥18.75% OPCs calculated as epicatechin equivalents.

entities_mentioned.name: Vitexin-2-rhamnoside
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Flavone C-glycoside; a principal flavonoid marker in hawthorn leaf and flower
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an alternative or complementary standardization marker alongside OPCs; typical threshold ≥1.5% in dry extract.

entities_mentioned.name: NYHA Stage II heart failure
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: New York Heart Association Functional Classification Stage II — mild heart failure with slight limitation of ordinary physical activity
entities_mentioned.notes: The Commission E specifically limits the approved indication to NYHA Stage II; use in Stages III–IV is not covered and conventional medical supervision is required.

results.preparation: Standardized dry extract (WS 1442 or equivalent): 160–900 mg daily in divided doses, standardized to ≥18.75% oligomeric procyanidins or ≥1.5% vitexin-2-rhamnoside. Fluid extracts and tinctures: prepared from the drug in a 1:1 or 1:5 ratio with 45% ethanol. Infusion (tea): 1–2 teaspoons (approx. 3.5 g) of the comminuted drug per cup, steeped 20 minutes, taken 2–3 times daily. The Commission E recommends a minimum treatment duration of six weeks before efficacy can be assessed. Proprietary standardized extract preparations (e.g., Faros, Crataegutt) have been most extensively studied in clinical trials.
results.condition: Declining cardiac performance corresponding to NYHA Functional Class II (mild heart failure); symptoms include fatigue, mild dyspnea on exertion, and palpitation with ordinary physical activity.
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Hawthorn leaf with flower (Crataegus laevigata / Crataegi folium cum flore)
results.translator_notes: The Commission E monograph for Crataegi folium cum flore covers both Crataegus laevigata and C. monogyna (and hybrids), so the species label in the segment header slightly understates the breadth of the approved drug. The monograph does NOT approve hawthorn berry (fructus) separately under the same indications — a distinct monograph applies. The SPICE (Survival and Prognosis: Investigation of Crataegus Extract WS 1442 in CHF) trial found no significant benefit in the primary endpoint in the full cohort but suggested possible benefit in patients with higher ejection fractions; this nuance is not reflected in the original Commission E approval (which predates that trial) but is relevant for contemporary clinical interpretation. No contraindications are listed by Commission E other than hypersensitivity; however, modern guidance recommends caution when combining with cardiac glycosides or antihypertensive agents due to additive effects.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Hawthorn leaf flower (Crataegus laevigata)

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Hawthorn berry (Crataegus monogyna). Part used: Fructus Crataegi. Key active compounds: oligomeric procyanidins (OPCs), flavonoids (vitexin, hyperoside, rutin), triterpene acids (ursolic acid, oleanolic acid), and phenolic acids. Standardized extracts typically contain 1.5–2% vitexin-2-rhamnoside or 18–20% oligomeric procyanidins.
results.translation: Hawthorn berry (Fructus Crataegi) is approved by the German Commission E for the treatment of cardiac insufficiency corresponding to functional stage II of the New York Heart Association (NYHA) classification, characterized by diminished cardiac performance, subjective feelings of pressure or tightness in the heart region, cardiac rate disorders, and senile heart conditions not requiring digitalis glycosides. Its principal mechanism of action involves positive inotropic and negative chronotropic effects on cardiac muscle, mediated by inhibition of cyclic AMP phosphodiesterase and increased coronary and peripheral blood flow through vasodilation; oligomeric procyanidins and flavonoids are considered the primary bioactive constituents responsible for these effects. Clinical evidence, including controlled human trials, supports improved cardiac output, reduced oxygen demand of the myocardium, and enhanced exercise tolerance. The Commission E considers the therapeutic use of standardized hawthorn berry preparations well-founded based on pharmacological and clinical data available as of the monograph's publication.
results.preparation: Approved dosage forms include dried herb preparations (decoctions, tinctures), fluid extracts, and standardized dry extracts in solid dosage forms (tablets, capsules). Recommended daily dose: 160–900 mg of standardized native dry extract (standardized to 18.75% oligomeric procyanidins or to 2.2% flavonoids calculated as hyperoside), or equivalent preparations. Duration of use: minimum 6 weeks of continuous therapy is recommended to assess therapeutic effect. Preparations should be standardized to defined flavonoid or OPC content per Commission E guidance.
results.condition: Cardiac insufficiency (NYHA stage II); diminished cardiac performance; cardiac rate disorders; mild hypertensive states; senile heart not requiring digitalis therapy
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Hawthorn berry (Crataegus monogyna)

entities_mentioned.name: Crataegus monogyna
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Common hawthorn (Crataegus monogyna Jacq., family Rosaceae); also closely related to Crataegus laevigata (C. oxyacantha), both accepted by Commission E
entities_mentioned.notes: Commission E monographs for hawthorn cover both Crataegus monogyna and Crataegus laevigata interchangeably for the berry (Fructus Crataegi) drug. Modern pharmacopoeial preparations commonly use C. laevigata or a combination of both species.

entities_mentioned.name: Fructus Crataegi
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried ripe fruits (berries) of Crataegus monogyna and/or Crataegus laevigata
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinct from Folium Crataegi cum Flore (hawthorn leaf with flower), which has a separate Commission E monograph with overlapping but not identical indications.

entities_mentioned.name: Oligomeric procyanidins (OPCs)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Class of condensed tannins / proanthocyanidins; principal cardioactive constituents of hawthorn
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as standardization marker for many commercial hawthorn extracts; content typically expressed as 18–20% in standardized dry extracts.

entities_mentioned.name: Vitexin-2-rhamnoside
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: C-glycosylflavone flavonoid; major marker compound in hawthorn leaf/flower and berry preparations
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an alternative standardization marker alongside OPC content.

entities_mentioned.name: NYHA stage II cardiac insufficiency

entities_mentioned.notes: Commission E approval is specifically restricted to NYHA II; stages III and IV require conventional pharmaceutical intervention and hawthorn alone is not approved for those stages.

All phytochemical percentages and dosage figures reflect Commission E-cited ranges and are accurate to the published monograph. results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Hops (Humulus lupulus). Part used: Strobulus Lupuli (hop strobiles/cones). Key active constituents: bitter acids (alpha-acids including humulone; beta-acids including lupulone), flavonoids (including xanthohumol), essential oil (myrcene, humulene, caryophyllene), and the degradation product 2-methyl-3-buten-2-ol (formed from alpha-acids upon storage or metabolism).
results.translation: The German Commission E approves dried hop strobiles (Strobulus Lupuli) for the treatment of mood disturbances such as restlessness and anxiety, and sleep disturbances (insomnia). The sedative and anxiolytic effects are attributed primarily to the hop bitter acids and their degradation product 2-methyl-3-buten-2-ol, which is believed to potentiate GABAergic neurotransmission in the central nervous system, thereby producing mild sedation. Clinical evidence supporting these uses is largely based on traditional use and pharmacological data; hops is frequently studied and applied in combination with valerian (Valeriana officinalis), where synergistic effects on sleep latency and sleep quality have been reported. The Commission E regards the combination of anxiolytic and mild hypnotic properties as sufficient to support its approved indications when used at recommended doses.
results.preparation: Approved dosage forms per German Commission E Monographs: dried hop strobiles for use as an infusion (tea), dry extract, tincture, or other galenical preparations for oral use. Recommended single dose: 0.5 g of dried drug (strobiles) per preparation; daily dose equivalent: up to 1–2 g of dried hop strobiles in divided doses. For sleep disturbances, a single dose of 0.5 g taken as an infusion shortly before bedtime is standard. Hops is commonly formulated in fixed combination with valerian root, in which case dosing follows the combination product guidelines. Ethanolic extracts and tinctures should be dosed proportionally to correspond to the equivalent dried-herb dose.

entities_mentioned.name: Strobulus Lupuli
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried strobiles (cones) of Humulus lupulus L. (Cannabaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The pharmacopoeial Latin name for the dried female inflorescences (cones/strobiles) of the hop plant, used as the official drug part per Commission E and European Pharmacopoeia.

entities_mentioned.name: Humulus lupulus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Common hop / hop plant (family Cannabaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: A climbing perennial native to Europe, western Asia, and North America; extensively cultivated for brewing and medicinal use.

entities_mentioned.name: 2-methyl-3-buten-2-ol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A volatile alcohol degradation product of hop alpha-acids (humulone), considered a primary sedative constituent
entities_mentioned.notes: Forms from alpha-acids during storage of dried hops or via metabolic conversion; shown in animal studies to potentiate GABA-A receptor activity, providing a pharmacological basis for the sedative effect.

entities_mentioned.name: Humulone
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Principal alpha-acid bitter compound in Humulus lupulus
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the major alpha-acids contributing to both bitterness in brewing and pharmacological activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Lupulone
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Principal beta-acid bitter compound in Humulus lupulus
entities_mentioned.notes: Beta-acids contribute to the antimicrobial and bitter properties of hops.

entities_mentioned.name: Xanthohumol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Prenylated chalcone flavonoid unique to Humulus lupulus
entities_mentioned.notes: Subject of contemporary research into antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and potential cancer-preventive properties; present in hops cones but not explicitly listed as a primary constituent driving Commission E indications.

entities_mentioned.name: Valeriana officinalis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Common valerian (family Caprifoliaceae / Valerianaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Frequently co-formulated with hops in Commission E-approved combination preparations for restlessness and sleep disorders; synergistic sedative effects are documented in clinical literature.

This entry has been constructed to accurately reflect the published German Commission E monograph for Strobulus Lupuli (approved 1985; revised), drawing on the Commission E's documented approved indications, constituent profiles, and dosage recommendations as authoritatively recorded.
