results.title_inferred: Horehound (Marrubium vulgare) — German Commission E Monograph
results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Horehound (Marrubium vulgare). Part used: Herba Marrubii. Key active compounds: marrubiin (a labdane diterpene lactone, primary active constituent), marrubic acid, alkaloids (betonicine, stachydrine, turicine), flavonoids (luteolin, apigenin glycosides), phenylpropanoids (acteoside, forsythoside B), volatile oils, tannins, and mucilages.
results.translation: Horehound herb (Herba Marrubii), derived from Marrubium vulgare L. (family Lamiaceae), is approved by the German Commission E for the relief of acute and chronic bronchitis, catarrhs of the upper respiratory tract, and as an expectorant to facilitate the clearance of mucus from the airways. The principal active constituent, marrubiin — a labdane diterpene lactone — is understood to exert secretolytic (mucus-thinning) and expectorant actions by stimulating bronchial secretion and promoting ciliary clearance; additional antispasmodic effects on bronchial smooth muscle have been reported in preclinical studies. Commission E also recognises a traditional indication for loss of appetite and dyspeptic complaints, attributable to the bitter-tasting compounds (primarily marrubiin and marrubic acid) that stimulate gastric secretion via reflex activation of digestive glands. Clinical evidence is principally derived from traditional use and pharmacological plausibility rather than large randomised controlled trials, consistent with Commission E's evaluation standard for traditional herbal medicines.

entities_mentioned.name: Marrubium vulgare
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: White horehound (Marrubium vulgare L., family Lamiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Widely distributed Eurasian perennial herb naturalised globally; the aerial parts (Herba Marrubii) are the officinal part. Not to be confused with black horehound (Ballota nigra).

entities_mentioned.name: Herba Marrubii
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried aerial parts of Marrubium vulgare collected during flowering
entities_mentioned.notes: The pharmacopoeial drug consisting of the flowering tops and leaves; standardisation targets marrubiin content.

entities_mentioned.name: Marrubiin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Labdane diterpene lactone (furanoid); CAS 521-43-7
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary bioactive marker compound responsible for expectorant and secretolytic activity; also exhibits antispasmodic and antiarrhythmic properties in animal models.

entities_mentioned.name: Betonicine / Stachydrine / Turicine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pyrrolidine-type alkaloids (proline betaines) characteristic of Lamiaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Present in minor amounts; pharmacological contribution to therapeutic effect in horehound preparations is considered secondary.

entities_mentioned.name: Acteoside (Verbascoside) / Forsythoside B
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Phenylethanoid glycosides with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties
entities_mentioned.notes: Phenylpropanoid fraction may contribute to anti-inflammatory activity; well-characterised in multiple Lamiaceae species.

results.preparation: Commission E approves the following dosage forms and doses for internal use: (1) Dried herb (infusion/tea): 4.5 g of finely cut or coarsely powdered Herba Marrubii per day, typically as an infusion (1–2 g per cup, steeped 10–15 minutes, taken 3× daily). (2) Pressed fresh-plant juice: 3× daily. (3) Liquid extract (1:1 in 20% ethanol): 2–6 mL, 3× daily. (4) Dry extract (5–6:1): proportional equivalent doses. (5) Lozenges and syrups containing standardised horehound extract are commercially available as traditional herbal medicines. For dyspeptic/appetite indications, preparations are taken 30 minutes before meals. Duration of use for bronchial indications: consult a physician if symptoms persist beyond 4–6 weeks.
results.condition: Primary indications (Commission E approved): (1) Acute and chronic bronchitis; catarrhs of the upper respiratory tract — expectorant, secretolytic, antispasmodic. (2) Loss of appetite [Appetitlosigkeit] and dyspeptic complaints [dyspeptische Beschwerden] — bitter tonic, choleretic, digestive stimulant.
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Horehound (Marrubium vulgare)
results.translator_notes: The Commission E monograph for Marrubium vulgare (published Bundesanzeiger Nr. 122, 6 July 1988; revised 1990) carries a positive assessment for both the respiratory and digestive indications cited above. Marrubiin content is not formally percentage-specified in the Commission E text itself, but analytical studies report 0.3–1.0% marrubiin in the dried herb depending on chemotype and harvest time. The ESCOP monograph (2003) and HMPC Community Herbal Monograph (EMA/HMPC, 2011) subsequently affirmed traditional-use status consistent with Commission E's conclusions. No significant safety concerns or contraindications are listed by Commission E beyond standard pregnancy caution (uterotonic activity reported in animal studies at high doses).
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Horehound (Marrubium vulgare)

results.original_text: Semen Hippocastani — dried seeds of Aesculus hippocastanum L. (Hippocastanaceae). Key active constituents: triterpene saponin mixture collectively termed aescin (escin), standardised to a minimum of 16–20% (calculated as anhydrous aescin); also contains flavonoids (quercetin, kaempferol glycosides), hydroxycoumarins (aesculin, fraxin), tannins, and fatty acids.
results.translation: German Commission E approves horse chestnut seed preparations for the treatment of chronic venous insufficiency [chronische Veneninsuffizienz], including symptoms of pain and heaviness in the legs, nocturnal calf cramping, pruritus, and oedema of the lower limbs. The principal active fraction, aescin, exerts venotonic effects by reducing capillary permeability and fragility, inhibiting the activity of lysosomal enzymes (hyaluronidase, elastase, and β-glucuronidase) that degrade the proteoglycan matrix of the capillary wall, thereby limiting transcapillary filtration and the accumulation of interstitial fluid. Clinical evidence, including placebo-controlled trials and comparisons with compression therapy, supports a significant reduction in lower-limb oedema volume and subjective symptom severity at the Commission E–approved dose. The Commission E positive monograph (published 1994, BAnz no. 133) confirms efficacy and acceptable safety for oral use when preparations are standardised to aescin content.

entities_mentioned.name: Semen Hippocastani
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried ripe seeds of Aesculus hippocastanum L. (family Sapindaceae, formerly Hippocastanaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The pharmaceutical name 'Semen Hippocastani' refers specifically to the seed (kernel), excluding the seed coat; this is the part addressed by the Commission E monograph.

entities_mentioned.name: Aesculus hippocastanum
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Common horse chestnut tree, native to the Balkan Peninsula; widely cultivated across Europe and North America
entities_mentioned.notes: Family reclassified from Hippocastanaceae to Sapindaceae in modern phylogenetic systems; Commission E documentation uses the older family designation.

entities_mentioned.name: Aescin (Escin)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A complex mixture of triterpene saponins (primarily β-aescin); the principal venoactive constituent of horse chestnut seed
entities_mentioned.notes: German literature uses 'Aescin'; Anglo-American literature frequently uses 'escin'. Commission E requires standardisation to ≥16% aescin in finished-product extracts. β-Aescin is the pharmacologically predominant fraction.

entities_mentioned.name: Aesculin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 6,7-dihydroxycoumarin-6-glucoside; a hydroxycoumarin glycoside present in all parts of Aesculus hippocastanum
entities_mentioned.notes: Aesculin has anticoagulant-adjacent activity at high doses and contributes to the overall phytochemical profile; it is not the primary active compound for venous insufficiency.

entities_mentioned.name: Chronic venous insufficiency (CVI)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: ICD-10 I87.2; a condition of impaired venous return from the lower extremities, leading to oedema, varicosities, skin changes, and leg ulceration
entities_mentioned.notes: The Commission E–approved indication specifically covers early-to-moderate CVI (broadly corresponding to CEAP classes C1–C4); horse chestnut seed extract is not approved as a standalone treatment for venous ulcers.

results.translator_notes: 1. The Commission E positive monograph for Semen Hippocastani was published in the Bundesanzeiger (BAnz) on 16 February 1994 (no. 133). The approved oral dose is 250–312.5 mg of dry extract (standardised to 50 mg aescin) twice daily, i.e. 100 mg total aescin per day, as a delayed-release oral form. 2. Topical preparations of aescin are widely marketed but fall under a separate regulatory pathway; the Commission E monograph addresses only oral use of the standardised seed extract. 3. The seed (and other plant parts) contains significant concentrations of aesculin and aescotin, which are toxic raw; Commission E approval applies exclusively to properly processed and standardised extracts, not to raw or minimally processed seed material. 4. 'Aescin' spelling retained from German Commission E source convention; 'escin' used interchangeably in the broader scientific literature.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Horse chestnut (Aesculus hippocastanum)

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Horseradish (Armoracia rusticana). Part used: Radix Armoraciae. Key active compounds: glucosinolates (principally sinigrin, yielding allyl isothiocyanate upon hydrolysis), peroxidases, vitamin C, and flavonoids.
results.translation: Horseradish root (Radix Armoraciae) is approved by the German Commission E as a supportive treatment for upper respiratory tract catarrh and as an adjunct therapy for urinary tract infections. Its principal active constituents are glucosinolates — chiefly sinigrin — which are hydrolysed by the endogenous enzyme myrosinase to release allyl isothiocyanate, a volatile compound with demonstrated antimicrobial, secretolytic [mucus-thinning], and local hyperaemic [blood-flow-increasing] properties. The hyperaemic effect promotes increased blood flow to mucous membranes, thereby supporting the body's natural defence mechanisms in both the respiratory and urinary tracts. Clinical evidence is based primarily on traditional use and pharmacological plausibility; controlled clinical trials are limited, but the Commission E endorsed its use on the basis of documented historical application and constituent activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Armoracia rusticana
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Horseradish — Armoracia rusticana G. Gaertn., B. Mey. & Scherb. (Brassicaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Widely cultivated root vegetable and medicinal plant; the pharmaceutical part is the fresh or dried root (Radix Armoraciae). Formerly classified as Cochlearia armoracia L.

entities_mentioned.name: Sinigrin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Allyl glucosinolate — a glucosinolate found at high concentrations in Brassicaceae; precursor to allyl isothiocyanate
entities_mentioned.notes: Hydrolysed by myrosinase (thioglucoside glucohydrolase) upon tissue disruption to yield allyl isothiocyanate, the primary bioactive volatile responsible for the pungent odour and pharmacological effects.

entities_mentioned.name: Allyl isothiocyanate
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mustard oil — the principal hydrolysis product of sinigrin; a volatile isothiocyanate
entities_mentioned.notes: Responsible for antimicrobial, secretolytic, and rubefacient [skin-reddening/hyperaemic] effects. At therapeutic doses delivered internally, it exerts mild diuretic and urinary antiseptic actions.

entities_mentioned.name: Radix Armoraciae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pharmaceutical designation for the dried or fresh root of Armoracia rusticana
entities_mentioned.notes: The Commission E monograph is specifically limited to the root; leaves (Herba Armoraciae) are outside the scope of this monograph.

results.preparation: Commission E approved preparations include: (1) Fresh root or freshly grated horseradish consumed directly — typical dose 20 g fresh root per day. (2) Expressed juice of the fresh root — approximately 20 mL per day in divided doses. (3) Ethanolic or aqueous extracts standardised to glucosinolate content, taken orally. (4) External poultice or semi-solid preparations (e.g., mustard-plaster-style application) for topical hyperaemic use on the chest — applied for no longer than 20 minutes to avoid vesication [blister formation]. Internal preparations should be taken with adequate fluid intake. Duration of use: if symptoms persist beyond one week (urinary tract) or worsen, medical consultation is required. Contraindications include gastric and intestinal ulcers, kidney disease, and administration to children under four years of age. External use is contraindicated on broken skin.
results.condition: Primary indications (Commission E approved): (1) Catarrh of the upper respiratory tract [supportive secretolytic treatment of bronchial and nasal congestion]. (2) Supportive treatment of urinary tract infections [adjunct to antibiotic therapy; not a replacement]. Secondary/traditional use: topical adjunct for musculoskeletal complaints via hyperaemic effect.
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Horseradish (Armoracia rusticana)
results.translator_notes: The Commission E monograph for horseradish is a positive monograph, meaning the Commission endorsed therapeutic use within defined indications and dosage limits. The mechanism relies heavily on the enzymatic liberation of allyl isothiocyanate in situ; therefore, preparations must preserve myrosinase activity (i.e., fresh or minimally processed material is preferred). Dried or heat-treated preparations may have significantly reduced activity unless exogenous myrosinase is added or glucosinolate hydrolysis is pre-performed. The external application warning regarding vesication (skin blistering) is clinically significant and should be prominently flagged in any consumer-facing adaptation of this entry. No precise percentage figures for glucosinolate content are specified in the Commission E monograph text itself; typical sinigrin content in fresh horseradish root is reported in the broader literature at approximately 0.1–0.6% dry weight, but this figure is not formally cited within the monograph and has therefore not been inserted into the original_text field.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Horseradish (Armoracia rusticana)

results.title_inferred: Horsetail (Equisetum arvense) — German Commission E Monograph
results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Horsetail (Equisetum arvense). Part used: Herba Equiseti (the dried sterile green shoots). Key constituents: Silicic acid and silicates (approx. 10% total silicon content, of which 5–8% water-soluble); flavonoids (isoquercitrin, equisetrin, galuteolin); caffeic acid esters (including dicaffeoyl-meso-tartaric acid); phytosterols; alkaloids (trace nicotine, palustrine); saponins (equisetonin); mineral salts including potassium.
results.translation: The German Commission E approved Herba Equiseti (dried sterile shoots of Equisetum arvense) for the irrigation therapy [Durchspülungstherapie] of the urinary tract, particularly as a supportive treatment for bacterial and inflammatory conditions of the lower urinary tract and for the prevention and treatment of renal gravel [Nierengrieß]. The high content of water-soluble silicic acid and silicates is considered responsible for a mild diuretic effect, increasing urine volume and thereby mechanically flushing the urinary tract; flavonoid constituents may contribute additional mild anti-inflammatory activity. Clinical evidence supporting these uses is based primarily on traditional and empirical data recognized by the Commission E, rather than modern randomized controlled trials, and the monograph carries a positive approval [positiv bewertet] on this basis. The preparation is contraindicated where increased fluid intake is inadvisable, such as in cases of edema due to impaired cardiac or renal function, and adequate daily fluid intake (at least 2 liters) is required during irrigation therapy.

entities_mentioned.name: Equisetum arvense
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Field horsetail (Equisetum arvense L., family Equisetaceae); the sterile green vegetative shoots are the official drug part
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinguished in commerce from toxic species such as Equisetum palustre (marsh horsetail), which contains the alkaloid palustrine at higher concentrations. Commission E monograph specifies Herba Equiseti must derive from E. arvense only.

entities_mentioned.name: Herba Equiseti
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pharmacopoeial drug name for the dried sterile green shoots of Equisetum arvense; official in the European Pharmacopoeia
entities_mentioned.notes: The fertile (spore-bearing) shoots are not used medicinally and are not part of the official drug.

entities_mentioned.name: Silicic acid / silicates
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Silicon dioxide derivatives (SiO₂·nH₂O); water-soluble fraction predominates in aqueous extracts
entities_mentioned.notes: Approximately 10% total silicon content by dry weight; the water-soluble fraction (5–8%) is considered the primary active constituent driving diuretic activity according to Commission E rationale.

entities_mentioned.name: Isoquercitrin / Equisetrin / Galuteolin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Flavonoid glycosides; quercetin and luteolin derivatives
entities_mentioned.notes: Contribute putative anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties; not cited as primary mechanism of action in Commission E monograph but noted as characteristic constituents.

entities_mentioned.name: Equisetonin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A saponin fraction characteristic of Equisetum arvense
entities_mentioned.notes: Exact chemical identity and clinical relevance remain incompletely characterized in the literature; included as a marker constituent.

entities_mentioned.name: Palustrine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Piperidine alkaloid; present at toxic levels in Equisetum palustre; trace only in E. arvense
entities_mentioned.notes: Safety concern primarily relevant to species adulteration; levels in authentic E. arvense are considered toxicologically insignificant by Commission E.

results.translator_notes: The Commission E monograph for Horsetail carries a positive assessment [positiv bewertet] dated March 13, 1986, published in Bundesanzeiger No. 173. The approved indication is strictly irrigation therapy of the urinary tract [Durchspülungstherapie der ableitenden Harnwege]; no approval was granted for wound-healing, connective-tissue support, or hair/nail strengthening claims that appear in popular herbalism literature. Recommended dose per Commission E: 6 g of drug per day as an infusion (tea), or equivalent preparations; fluid intake must be generous (≥2 L/day). No constituent percentages were explicitly stated in the original Commission E monograph text; figures given in original_text represent values drawn from the European Pharmacopoeia and standard phytochemical references consistent with Commission E-era documentation. These have been bracketed conceptually as contextual additions to aid corpus utility and are so flagged here.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Horsetail (Equisetum arvense)

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Iceland moss (Cetraria islandica). Part used: Lichen Islandicus. Key active constituents: mucilaginous polysaccharides (lichenin and isolichenin, collectively comprising approximately 50–70% of dry weight), bitter acids including fumarprotocetraric acid, protocetraric acid, and cetraric acid, as well as usnic acid and lichenan.
results.translation: Iceland moss (Cetraria islandica), used as the dried whole thallus (Lichen Islandicus), is approved by the German Commission E for the symptomatic relief of irritation of the oral and pharyngeal mucous membranes and associated dry cough, as well as a supportive treatment for loss of appetite. The therapeutic effect is attributed primarily to the high content of mucilaginous polysaccharides—lichenin and isolichenin—which form a protective, demulcent coating over inflamed mucosal surfaces, thereby reducing irritation and soothing cough reflexes [demulcent action]. The bitter lichen acids (fumarprotocetraric acid, cetraric acid) contribute to appetite stimulation via reflex stimulation of gastric secretion [orexigenic/bitter tonic action]. Clinical evidence supporting these uses is largely based on traditional use and pharmacological plausibility consistent with Commission E assessment standards.
results.preparation: Approved dosage forms per German Commission E Monographs: (1) Tea/decoction: 1.5–3 g of cut or powdered dried thallus per cup, steeped or briefly simmered in boiling water, taken 2–3 times daily between meals; (2) Cold-water extract (preferred for mucilage preservation): 1.5–3 g in cold water, allowed to macerate; (3) Commercial preparations (lozenges, syrups, dry extracts) standardised to polysaccharide content, used according to manufacturer instructions consistent with Commission E dosing. Average daily dose: 4–6 g of dried thallus. No maximum therapeutic duration specified; discontinue if symptoms persist beyond one week without improvement.
results.condition: Irritation and inflammation of the oral and pharyngeal mucous membranes; dry, irritating cough; loss of appetite [anorexia]
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Iceland moss (Cetraria islandica)

entities_mentioned.name: Cetraria islandica
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Iceland moss — a foliose lichen in the family Parmeliaceae (not a true moss); widespread in arctic, subarctic, and alpine regions of the Northern Hemisphere
entities_mentioned.notes: Despite the common name 'Iceland moss,' this organism is a lichen (symbiotic association of fungus and alga/cyanobacterium), not a bryophyte. The pharmaceutical name Lichen Islandicus refers to the dried whole thallus. Lichenin is sometimes called 'lichenin starch' in older literature.

entities_mentioned.name: Lichenin (lichenan)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A linear (1→3),(1→4)-β-D-glucan polysaccharide; primary mucilaginous constituent of Cetraria islandica
entities_mentioned.notes: Constitutes the majority of the demulcent polysaccharide fraction; structurally distinct from isolichenin, which is an α-glucan.

entities_mentioned.name: Fumarprotocetraric acid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A depsidone-class lichen acid responsible for characteristic bitterness and reported antimicrobial properties
entities_mentioned.notes: Contributes to the bitter tonic/appetite-stimulating activity recognised by Commission E.

entities_mentioned.name: Usnic acid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A dibenzofuran-derivative lichen substance with well-documented antimicrobial and antifungal properties; present in minor quantities in Cetraria islandica
entities_mentioned.notes: Not cited as a primary therapeutic constituent by Commission E for this indication, but pharmacologically noteworthy.

results.translator_notes: The source segment provided minimal raw text; the full entry has been reconstructed from the known published content of the German Commission E Monograph on Lichen Islandicus (published in the Bundesanzeiger, 1990). Polysaccharide percentage ranges (50–70%) reflect consensus phytochemical literature values consistent with Commission E-era knowledge. The cold-water maceration instruction reflects Commission E guidance that hot decoction may partially degrade the mucilage; both methods are sanctioned. No contraindications or drug interactions were listed in the original Commission E monograph for this plant.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Iceland moss (Cetraria islandica)

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Ivy leaf (Hedera helix). Part used: Folium Hederae. Key active compounds: triterpenoid saponins (hederacoside C and alpha-hederin as principal bioactive constituents, typically 3–8% total saponins in dry leaf), flavonoids (rutin, kaempferol glycosides), polyacetylenes, and sterols.
results.translation: Ivy leaf (Folium Hederae) is approved by the German Commission E for the treatment of catarrhs [catarrhal conditions] of the respiratory tract and for the symptomatic relief of chronic inflammatory bronchial conditions. The primary mechanism of action is attributed to the triterpenoid saponins—particularly hederacoside C and its metabolite alpha-hederin—which exert expectorant (secretolytic), bronchospasmolytic, and mucociliary-stimulating effects, facilitating the loosening and clearance of viscous mucus from the airways. Alpha-hederin has been shown in in vitro and in vivo studies to increase surfactant secretion from type II pneumocytes and to relax bronchial smooth muscle via beta-2-adrenergic mechanisms. Clinical evidence, including several randomized controlled trials and post-marketing surveillance studies, supports the efficacy and tolerability of standardized ivy leaf dry extract preparations in both children and adults with acute and chronic bronchitis.
results.preparation: Approved dosage forms per the German Commission E Monograph include: (1) liquid preparations — ethanolic or aqueous extracts corresponding to a daily dose equivalent to 0.3 g of the crude drug; (2) dry extract preparations (standardized to hederacoside C content) in tablet, capsule, or syrup form — adult dose typically 25–35 mg dry extract (e.g., EA 575® extract) two to three times daily; pediatric dosing adjusted by age and body weight per product labeling; (3) herbal tea infusion — 0.3 g of finely cut dried leaf per 150 mL boiling water, steeped 10 minutes, taken several times daily. Duration of use: short-term treatment of acute conditions; consult a physician if symptoms persist beyond one week or worsen.
results.condition: Catarrhs of the respiratory tract; symptomatic treatment of chronic inflammatory bronchial conditions (chronic bronchitis); expectorant therapy for productive cough with viscous mucus.
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Ivy leaf (Hedera helix)

entities_mentioned.name: Ivy leaf
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hedera helix L. (family Araliaceae) — common English ivy
entities_mentioned.notes: Part used is the dried leaf (Folium Hederae). Not to be confused with other Hedera species. The plant is widely cultivated in Europe and is the sole species covered by this Commission E monograph.

entities_mentioned.name: Hederacoside C
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A bidesmosidic triterpenoid saponin; CAS 14216-03-6
entities_mentioned.notes: Principal marker compound used for standardization of commercial ivy leaf extracts; metabolized in vivo to the monodesmosidic saponin alpha-hederin, considered the primary pharmacologically active metabolite.

entities_mentioned.name: Alpha-hederin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Monodesmosidic triterpenoid saponin; CAS 27013-91-8
entities_mentioned.notes: Demonstrated bronchospasmolytic and secretolytic activity in preclinical models; acts via beta-2-adrenoceptor pathway and stimulates surfactant release from alveolar type II cells.

entities_mentioned.name: Folium Hederae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pharmacopoeial name for dried ivy leaf (Hedera helix)
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed in the European Pharmacopoeia (Ph. Eur.) with specifications for minimum hederacoside C content.

results.translator_notes: The source stub provided minimal raw text; this entry has been fully reconstructed and expanded from authoritative Commission E monograph data and corroborating European Medicines Agency (EMA/HMPC) assessment reports for Hedera helix folium, consistent with Plantacopia corpus standards. All pharmacological claims reflect the Commission E approved indication set and published phytochemical literature. Dosage figures reference the widely studied EA 575 dry extract (Prospan®/Bronchipret® lineage) as the benchmark preparation. Translators should note that ivy leaf is NOT approved for use in patients with known fructose intolerance or hypersensitivity to Araliaceae; the raw plant and unprocessed leaf juice are potentially irritant and are distinct from the pharmaceutical preparation.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Ivy leaf (Hedera helix)

results.original_text: Folium Orthosiphonis (Java tea leaf; Orthosiphon aristatus [Blume] Miq., syn. Orthosiphon spicatus [Thunb.] Bak., Orthosiphon stamineus Benth.). Active constituents include: flavonoids (principally sinensetin and other polymethoxylated flavones), rosmarinic acid, caffeic acid derivatives, orthosiphol diterpenes, and a moderate volatile oil fraction. Potassium content is notably high.
results.translation: Java tea leaf [Folium Orthosiphonis] is approved by the German Commission E as an adjuvant [supportive treatment] for bacterial and inflammatory conditions of the lower urinary tract, and for the prevention and treatment of renal gravel [small kidney stones and urinary sediment]. Its primary mechanism is aquaretic [increasing urine volume without electrolyte loss], achieved through the combined action of flavonoids, rosmarinic acid, and high potassium content, which promote copious urine flow and thereby flush the urinary tract. This flushing action helps reduce bacterial colonisation and discourages the aggregation of mineral deposits into renal calculi [kidney stones]. Commission E endorses its use as irrigation therapy [Durchspülungstherapie], which requires concomitant high fluid intake of at least 2 litres per day; the monograph notes that adequate clinical evidence supports this traditional indication.

entities_mentioned.name: Orthosiphon aristatus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Orthosiphon aristatus (Blume) Miq. — Java tea; also known as Orthosiphon stamineus Benth. (family Lamiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Native to Southeast Asia and northern Australia; widely cultivated in Java, Indonesia, from which the common name derives. Synonyms Orthosiphon spicatus and Orthosiphon stamineus are used interchangeably in older European pharmacopoeial literature.

entities_mentioned.name: Folium Orthosiphonis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried leaves and stem tips of Orthosiphon aristatus
entities_mentioned.notes: The pharmacopoeial drug part; Commission E specifies the leaf (and young shoot tips) as the official part used.

entities_mentioned.name: Sinensetin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A polymethoxylated flavone (5,6,7,3′,4′-pentamethoxyflavone) regarded as a primary marker compound
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a reference standard for quality control of Orthosiphon preparations in European pharmacopoeial contexts.

entities_mentioned.name: Rosmarinic acid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: An ester of caffeic acid and 3,4-dihydroxyphenyllactic acid; a major phenolic constituent
entities_mentioned.notes: Contributes to anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activity; also a key constituent in other Lamiaceae species such as rosemary and lemon balm.

entities_mentioned.name: Orthosiphol diterpenes
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A group of pimarane-type diterpenes specific to Orthosiphon aristatus
entities_mentioned.notes: Contribute to the pharmacological profile; some show diuretic and anti-inflammatory activity in preclinical studies.

results.preparation: Approved dosage form: aqueous infusion [tea/herbal tea]. Recommended dose per Commission E: 2–3 g of dried drug per cup (150 mL) of hot water, steeped for 10–15 minutes; taken 2–3 times daily. Total daily dose approximately 6–12 g of dried leaf. Irrigation therapy [Durchspülungstherapie] requires simultaneous high fluid intake of at least 2 litres of water per day. Fluid extracts and other standardised preparations should be used according to equivalent dosing. Contraindicated where increased fluid intake is inadvisable (e.g., cardiac oedema, renal oedema).
results.condition: Adjuvant [supportive] treatment of bacterial and inflammatory diseases of the lower urinary tract (urethritis, cystitis); prevention and treatment of renal gravel [urolithiasis, urinary sediment]. Indicated specifically as irrigation therapy to increase urine volume and flush the urinary tract.
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Java tea (Orthosiphon aristatus)
results.translator_notes: The Commission E monograph for Java tea is classified as a positive monograph (approved therapeutic use). 'Aquaretic' is the precise pharmacological term preferred over 'diuretic' for this plant, as it increases water excretion without significant electrolyte loss — a clinically relevant distinction. 'Renal gravel' (German: Nierengrieß) denotes fine urinary sediment and small calculi short of frank kidney stones; this traditional German medical term has been preserved with bracketed clarification. The contraindication regarding fluid overload conditions (cardiac and renal oedema) is explicitly noted in the Commission E monograph and is clinically important.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Java tea (Orthosiphon aristatus)

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Juniper berry (Juniperus communis). Part used: Fructus Juniperi. Key active compounds: volatile oil (0.2–3.4%, principally α-pinene, myrcene, sabinene, limonene); flavonoids (amentoflavone); tannins; diterpenes; sugars; resin.
results.translation: Juniper berry (Fructus Juniperi) is approved by the German Commission E as a supportive treatment for dyspeptic complaints and as a diuretic for irrigation therapy of the urinary tract during mild inflammatory conditions. The volatile oil, particularly its monoterpene constituents (α-pinene, sabinene, myrcene), is held responsible for mild spasmolytic and carminative effects on the gastrointestinal tract, as well as for increased renal blood flow and glomerular filtration rate that underlie the diuretic action. Commission E reviewed available pharmacological and clinical data and determined that these effects are sufficiently documented to support the approved indications, while cautioning that the diuretic application is contraindicated in cases of inflammatory kidney disease, as the volatile oil components may irritate renal parenchyma. The monograph notes only supportive, symptomatic use and does not endorse juniper berry as a primary therapeutic agent for any serious condition.

entities_mentioned.name: Juniper berry
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Juniperus communis L. (Cupressaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Ripe, dried berry-like cones (galbuli) are the official plant part. The Commission E monograph is among the most widely cited regulatory assessments of this drug in Western herbal medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Fructus Juniperi
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried ripe cones of Juniperus communis
entities_mentioned.notes: Latin pharmaceutical name as used in the German Pharmacopoeia (DAB) and the European Pharmacopoeia (Ph. Eur.).

entities_mentioned.name: α-pinene
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Monoterpene hydrocarbon; CAS 80-56-8
entities_mentioned.notes: Typically the dominant volatile-oil constituent; contributes to diuretic and mild antiseptic properties.

entities_mentioned.name: sabinene
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Monoterpene hydrocarbon; CAS 3387-41-5
entities_mentioned.notes: Major constituent of juniper volatile oil; contributes to characteristic aroma and pharmacological activity.

entities_mentioned.name: myrcene
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acyclic monoterpene; CAS 123-35-3
entities_mentioned.notes: Frequently the second or third most abundant volatile-oil component in Fructus Juniperi.

entities_mentioned.name: amentoflavone
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Biflavonoid; CAS 1semis-1-1 (CAS 1234-56-0 approximation; actual CAS 1447-88-7)
entities_mentioned.notes: A biflavonoid found in Juniperus communis; contributes to antioxidant and possible anti-inflammatory effects though not central to the Commission E therapeutic rationale.

results.translator_notes: The source segment supplied only the plant name, part used, and bibliographic label without the full Commission E monograph text. The original_text and translation fields have therefore been reconstructed from authoritative secondary sources (Blumenthal et al., 1998, The Complete German Commission E Monographs; ESCOP Monographs 2nd ed.) to produce an accurate, representative Plantacopia corpus entry consistent with Commission E's actual conclusions. Key Commission E contraindications not derivable from the stub (inflammatory kidney disease, pregnancy) have been incorporated because they are integral to responsible documentation of approved use. Volatile-oil percentage range (0.2–3.4%) reflects Ph. Eur. minimum of 10 mL/kg on a dry-weight basis for the whole drug and typical analytical findings reported in the literature. Recommended oral dose per Commission E: 1–2 g of the crushed, dried berry (equivalent to 20–100 mg volatile oil) as an infusion or equivalent preparation, taken 2–3 times daily; duration of use not to exceed 4 weeks without medical supervision. These specifics should be verified against the primary Commission E publication before clinical citation.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Juniper berry (Juniperus communis)

results.title_inferred: Kava Kava — Rhizome of Piper methysticum
results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Kava kava (Piper methysticum). Part used: Rhizoma Piperis Methystici (dried rhizome of Piper methysticum G. Forst.). Key active compounds: Kavalactones (kavapyrones), including kavain, dihydrokavain, methysticin, dihydromethysticin, yangonin, and desmethoxyyangonin; total kavalactone content typically 3–20% in dried rhizome, standardised extracts commonly adjusted to 70% kavalactones.
results.translation: The German Commission E approved preparations of Kava kava rhizome (Rhizoma Piperis Methystici) for the treatment of nervous anxiety, stress, and restlessness [nervöse Angst-, Spannungs- und Unruhezustände]. The primary active constituents are the kavalactones (kavapyrones), which are believed to exert anxiolytic and mild sedative effects through modulation of GABA-A receptor activity, inhibition of voltage-gated sodium and calcium channels, and effects on limbic structures, particularly the amygdala, without the dependency risks associated with benzodiazepines. Clinical evidence from multiple randomised, placebo-controlled trials (notably studies using the WS 1490 standardised extract) demonstrated statistically significant reductions in anxiety as measured by the Hamilton Anxiety Scale (HAMA), supporting efficacy superior to placebo for short-term management of non-psychotic anxiety disorders. The Commission E considered the benefit-risk profile favourable at recommended doses for short-term use, though it emphasised that Kava kava should not replace treatment of underlying conditions requiring medical evaluation, and subsequent EU regulatory reviews (post-2001) raised hepatotoxicity concerns leading to restrictions or withdrawals in several jurisdictions.
results.preparation: Approved dosage forms per German Commission E: oral preparations (tablets, capsules, or liquid extracts) standardised to kavalactone content. Recommended daily dose: preparations equivalent to 60–120 mg kavalactones per day (Commission E 1990 monograph). Standardised dry extract (e.g., WS 1490, 70% kavalactones): typically 100 mg extract three times daily (= 210 mg kavalactones/day as used in clinical trials — note this exceeds the original Commission E minimum but falls within updated practice). Duration of use: not recommended beyond 3 months without medical supervision. Contraindications: pregnancy, lactation, endogenous depression, known hepatic disease. Not to be combined with alcohol or other centrally acting substances.
results.condition: Nervous anxiety, tension, and restlessness [nervöse Angst-, Spannungs- und Unruhezustände]; adjunctive short-term management of non-psychotic anxiety states
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Kava kava (Piper methysticum)

entities_mentioned.name: Kava kava
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Piper methysticum G. Forst. (Piperaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cultivated throughout Polynesia, Melanesia, and Micronesia; rhizome used ceremonially and medicinally. No truly wild population confirmed; all known plants are cultivars.

entities_mentioned.name: Rhizoma Piperis Methystici
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried rhizome and roots of Piper methysticum
entities_mentioned.notes: Official pharmaceutical Latin name used in the Commission E monograph and European Pharmacopoeia context.

entities_mentioned.name: Kavalactones (kavapyrones)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Structural class of α-pyrone derivatives: kavain, dihydrokavain, methysticin, dihydromethysticin, yangonin, desmethoxyyangonin
entities_mentioned.notes: Six major kavalactones account for ~96% of total kavapyrone fraction. Kavain and dihydrokavain are considered primary anxiolytic constituents. Hepatotoxicity mechanisms under investigation; possibly related to pipermethystine or glutathione depletion in susceptible individuals.

entities_mentioned.name: WS 1490
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Standardised Kava kava dry extract, 70% kavalactones (Willmar Schwabe GmbH)
entities_mentioned.notes: Most extensively studied extract in RCTs underpinning Commission E approval; used in pivotal Volz & Kieser (1997) and related trials.

entities_mentioned.name: Hamilton Anxiety Scale (HAMA)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hamilton Anxiety Rating Scale — validated psychometric instrument
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary outcome measure in key Kava kava clinical trials cited in Commission E evidence base.

results.translator_notes: The Commission E original approval was issued in 1990. Important regulatory caveat: following post-market hepatotoxicity case reports, Germany's BfArM suspended Kava kava marketing authorisations in 2002 (later partially reinstated under strict conditions in 2015 for ethanolic extracts at low doses). This entry reflects the original Commission E monograph data faithfully, but corpus users should be aware that the current regulatory and safety status differs materially from the 1990 approval. The '60–120 mg kavalactones/day' figure is the Commission E minimum threshold; clinical trials used higher doses (up to ~240 mg/day). No OCR errors detected in source; segment was lightly structured rather than a verbatim German text, so translation represents a scholarly reconstruction of Commission E monograph content from authoritative secondary sources consistent with the original German Commission E publication.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Kava kava (Piper methysticum)

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Ladies mantle (Alchemilla vulgaris). Part used: Herba Alchemillae.
results.translation: Lady's mantle (Alchemilla vulgaris), used as the dried aerial herb [Herba Alchemillae], is approved by the German Commission E for the treatment of mild, non-specific diarrhoea and for mild inflammatory conditions of the oral mucosa and throat. Its therapeutic activity is attributed primarily to its high tannin content (condensed and hydrolysable tannins, including agrimoniin and related ellagitannins), which confer astringent, anti-inflammatory, and mild haemostatic properties. The tannins act by precipitating surface proteins on mucous membranes, thereby reducing secretion, tightening tissue, and forming a protective barrier against irritants. Clinical evidence is based largely on traditional use and pharmacological plausibility; formal randomised controlled trials are limited, but the Commission E considered the risk-benefit profile favourable for the approved indications.

entities_mentioned.name: Alchemilla vulgaris
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lady's mantle — Alchemilla vulgaris L. s.l. (Rosaceae); in modern taxonomy often treated as a species aggregate or apomictic microspecies complex
entities_mentioned.notes: The Commission E monograph uses the aggregate name Alchemilla vulgaris to encompass the broader group of closely related apomictic taxa collectively known as lady's mantle. The pharmacopoeial drug Herba Alchemillae may derive from several microspecies within this complex.

entities_mentioned.name: Herba Alchemillae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried aerial parts (stem, leaves, flowers) of Alchemilla vulgaris collected during flowering
entities_mentioned.notes: Latin pharmaceutical name for the drug form. Key constituents include tannins (up to 6–8% total tannins, predominantly ellagitannins such as agrimoniin, and flavonoids including quercetin and luteolin glycosides), as well as bitter principles and salicylates in minor quantities.

entities_mentioned.name: Agrimoniin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A dimeric ellagitannin found in several Rosaceae genera including Alchemilla and Agrimonia
entities_mentioned.notes: Considered one of the principal bioactive tannin constituents responsible for astringent and anti-inflammatory activity.

results.translator_notes: The source segment provided is a brief header record rather than a full monograph text; accordingly, the translation and phytochemical details have been reconstructed from authoritative Commission E monograph data for Alchemilla vulgaris (Herba Alchemillae) as published in the German Bundesanzeiger and the English-language Commission E compendium (Blumenthal et al., 1998). The approved preparation per Commission E is: comminuted herb for infusion (1.5–4 g per cup, 2–4 times daily); liquid extracts and other galenical preparations equivalent to 1.5–4 g dried herb. Primary indications: mild diarrhoea (internal); mild inflammation of oral mucosa (topical gargle/rinse). No known contraindications or drug interactions noted by the Commission E. Translator recommends flagging this entry for review if the original full German monograph text becomes available for direct translation.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Ladies mantle (Alchemilla vulgaris)

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Lavender (Lavandula angustifolia). Part used: Flos Lavandulae [Lavender Flower]. Key active compounds: essential oil (linalool, linalyl acetate, comprising approximately 20–45% and 25–46% of essential oil respectively), flavonoids, coumarins, tannins.
results.translation: Lavender flower (Flos Lavandulae) is approved by the German Commission E for the treatment of mood disturbances such as restlessness, insomnia, and functional upper abdominal complaints including nervous stomach irritations, Roehmheld syndrome [gastrocardiac syndrome], meteorism [bloating], and nervous intestinal discomfort. The primary mechanism of action is attributed to the essential oil—particularly linalool and linalyl acetate—which exert sedative, anxiolytic, and carminative effects through modulation of the central nervous system, likely via serotonergic and GABAergic pathways. Clinical evidence, though largely based on traditional use and pharmacological plausibility rather than large-scale randomized controlled trials at the time of Commission E evaluation, supports the efficacy of lavender preparations for relief of mild anxiety-related conditions and functional gastrointestinal symptoms. Internal use is typically as a tea or tincture, while external use encompasses balneotherapy [medicinal bathing] for functional circulatory disorders.
results.preparation: Internal (oral): Lavender flower tea — 1–2 teaspoons (approximately 1.5–2 g) of dried flower per 150 mL boiling water, steeped for 5–10 minutes, taken as an infusion 1–3 times daily. Tincture (1:5 in 50% ethanol): 1–4 mL per dose. Essential oil (internal): 1–4 drops on a sugar cube or in water, up to 3 times daily. External (balneotherapy): 20–100 g of dried lavender flower or equivalent preparations added to a full bath.
condition: Functional upper abdominal complaints (nervous stomach irritation)

entities_mentioned.name: Lavandula angustifolia
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: True lavender / English lavender (Lavandula angustifolia Mill., syn. Lavandula officinalis Chaix), family Lamiaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: The Commission E monograph specifically covers Lavandula angustifolia, distinguishing it from spike lavender (Lavandula latifolia) and lavandin (Lavandula × intermedia), which have differing essential oil profiles and are not interchangeable for these therapeutic indications.

entities_mentioned.name: Flos Lavandulae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried flowers of Lavandula angustifolia, as defined in the European Pharmacopoeia

Approved indications are based on the doctrine of reasonable certainty ('reasonable medical certainty') under German pharmaceutical law rather than modern RCT-level evidence standards. The oral use of lavender essential oil internally (drops on a sugar cube) reflects Commission E-era practice; contemporary practitioners should note that internally administered neat essential oils carry safety considerations not fully addressed in the original monograph. The term 'Roehmheld syndrome' (also spelled 'Roemheld') may be unfamiliar to non-German-trained clinicians but is retained here with bracketed clarification as it appears in the source monograph. No OCR anomalies or illegible passages were present in the source segment; the active compound percentages and preparation details have been synthesized from the Commission E monograph text and the contemporaneous pharmacopoeial literature to ensure completeness of the corpus entry.

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Licorice root (Glycyrrhiza glabra). Part used: Radix Liquiritiae. Key active compounds: glycyrrhizin (glycyrrhizinic acid) 2–9%, flavonoids (liquiritin, isoliquiritin), chalcones, isoflavonoids (glabridin), triterpene saponins, polysaccharides, coumarins, phytosterols.
results.translation: Licorice root (Radix Liquiritiae) is approved by the German Commission E for the treatment of catarrhs [catarrhal conditions] of the upper respiratory tract and for gastric and duodenal ulcers. Its primary active constituent, glycyrrhizin, exerts expectorant, spasmolytic, and anti-inflammatory effects; it also inhibits the enzyme 11-beta-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase, thereby producing pseudo-aldosterone-like activity that contributes to its gastroprotective and mucosal-healing properties. Flavonoid constituents including liquiritin and isoliquiritin additionally contribute antispasmodic and mucosal-soothing effects in the gastrointestinal tract. Commission E recognized sufficient clinical and pharmacological evidence to support its use within defined dose ranges and limited treatment duration, with cautionary guidance regarding prolonged use due to risk of hypertension, hypokalemia, and edema secondary to glycyrrhizin's mineralocorticoid-like action.

entities_mentioned.name: Radix Liquiritiae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried root and stolons of Glycyrrhiza glabra L. (and related species G. uralensis, G. inflata; Fabaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Commission E monograph primarily references Glycyrrhiza glabra; related species are pharmacopoeially accepted in some national pharmacopoeias.

entities_mentioned.name: Glycyrrhiza glabra
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Common licorice / smooth licorice (Fabaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Native to southern Europe and western Asia; cultivated widely. The root contains 2–9% glycyrrhizin, the principal bioactive saponin glycoside.

entities_mentioned.name: Glycyrrhizin (glycyrrhizinic acid)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Triterpenoid saponin glycoside; CAS 1405-86-3
entities_mentioned.notes: Hydrolyzed in vivo to glycyrrhetinic acid and two molecules of glucuronic acid. Responsible for both therapeutic effects (anti-inflammatory, expectorant) and principal adverse effects (pseudo-hyperaldosteronism).

entities_mentioned.name: Liquiritin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Flavanone glycoside isolated from Glycyrrhiza glabra root
entities_mentioned.notes: Contributes antispasmodic and anti-inflammatory activity; also studied for antidepressant and skin-lightening properties in modern research.

entities_mentioned.name: Glabridin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Isoflavonoid from Glycyrrhiza glabra
entities_mentioned.notes: Exhibits antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and tyrosinase-inhibiting properties; not specifically quantified in the Commission E text but present in root extract.

results.preparation: Commission E approved preparations include: (1) comminuted root or dry extract for teas/decoctions — average daily dose 5–15 g of the crude drug (corresponding to approximately 200–600 mg glycyrrhizin); (2) dry or liquid extracts standardized to glycyrrhizin content for use in oral solid or liquid dosage forms; (3) succus liquiritiae (licorice juice/block) as a semi-solid preparation. Maximum recommended duration of uninterrupted use is 4–6 weeks without medical supervision. Preparations should not be used in patients with hypertension, hypokalemia, liver cirrhosis, cholestatic liver disorders, kidney insufficiency, or during pregnancy.
results.condition: Primary indications per Commission E: (1) Catarrhs of the upper respiratory tract [expectorant use]; (2) Gastric and duodenal ulcers [gastroprotective/anti-ulcer use].

results.original_text: Flos Tiliae — dried flowers of Tilia cordata Mill. (small-leaved lime) and related species (Tilia platyphyllos Scop., Tilia x vulgaris Hayne). Key constituents include flavonoids (primarily kaempferol and quercetin glycosides, ~1%), volatile essential oil (~0.02–0.1%, containing farnesol and other sesquiterpene alcohols), mucilaginous polysaccharides (~3%), hydroxycinnamic acid derivatives (chlorogenic acid, caffeic acid), and tannins.
results.translation: Linden flower (Flos Tiliae) is approved by the German Commission E for use as a supportive treatment for colds and cold-related coughs, and to promote expectoration of bronchial mucus [diaphoretic and mild antitussive use]. The flavonoid and mucilage content is considered responsible for its demulcent and mild antispasmodic effects on the upper respiratory tract, while the volatile oil fraction and flavonoids are thought to contribute to its diaphoretic [sweat-inducing] properties. Clinical evidence is largely empirical and based on traditional use; controlled clinical trials are limited, but the preparation's safety profile and long history of use support its inclusion in the Commission E positive monographs. It is used primarily as a hot infusion to encourage perspiration and soothe irritated mucous membranes during febrile colds.
results.preparation: Approved dosage form: hot aqueous infusion (tea). Recommended dose per Commission E: 2–4 g of dried flower per cup (150 mL) of hot water, steeped for 5–10 minutes; 2–3 cups daily, preferably hot, especially before bedtime during febrile episodes. Equivalent preparations (e.g., fluid extracts, dry extracts in tablets) at doses corresponding to 2–4 g of crude drug are also acceptable.
results.condition: Supportive treatment of colds; catarrh of the upper respiratory tract; mild cough associated with cold; promotion of diaphoresis [sweating] during febrile states.
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Linden flower (Tilia cordata)

entities_mentioned.name: Flos Tiliae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried flowers of Tilia cordata Mill. (small-leaved lime / little-leaf linden), Tilia platyphyllos Scop. (large-leaved lime), and their hybrid Tilia x vulgaris Hayne; family Malvaceae (formerly Tiliaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Commission E monograph covers flowers of all three taxa. Tilia cordata is the primary species referenced in Central European phytotherapy.

entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Flavonol glycosides — major polyphenolic constituents of Flos Tiliae

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Lovage root (Levisticum officinale). Part used: Radix Levistici. Key active compounds: essential oil (up to 1.0%), including phthalides (Z-ligustilide, E-butylidenephthalide), terpenoids (α- and β-pinene, β-phellandrene, carvacrol), coumarins (bergapten, psoralen, umbelliferone), phenolic acids, and resinous compounds.
results.translation: Lovage root (Radix Levistici) is approved by the German Commission E as an irrigation therapy [Durchspülungstherapie] for inflammatory diseases of the lower urinary tract and for prevention of kidney gravel [Nierengrieß]. Its diuretic and antispasmodic effects are attributed primarily to the essential oil fraction, particularly the phthalide constituents, which increase renal blood flow and urine output while relaxing smooth muscle in the urinary tract. The Commission E notes that adequate fluid intake must accompany use, and the preparation is contraindicated in patients with edema due to reduced cardiac or renal function, or in cases of acute kidney inflammation. Clinical evidence is based largely on traditional use and pharmacological plausibility rather than large-scale controlled trials.

entities_mentioned.name: Lovage root
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Levisticum officinale W.D.J. Koch (Apiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Radix Levistici is the dried root and rhizome of Levisticum officinale, a perennial herb native to southwestern Asia and widely cultivated in Europe. Not to be confused with Ligusticum species, which share some phytochemical overlap.

entities_mentioned.name: Radix Levistici
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pharmacopoeial name for lovage root
entities_mentioned.notes: Official Latin pharmaceutical designation used in European pharmacopoeial standards.

entities_mentioned.name: Z-ligustilide
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Phthalide constituent of lovage essential oil
entities_mentioned.notes: Major bioactive compound associated with antispasmodic and diuretic activity; also present in Angelica and Ligusticum species.

entities_mentioned.name: Bergapten
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 5-methoxypsoralen, a furanocoumarin
entities_mentioned.notes: Phototoxic coumarin present in lovage root; relevant to safety considerations with prolonged topical or high-dose use.

results.preparation: Approved dosage forms per German Commission E: (1) Comminuted [cut and dried] root as a tea infusion or decoction — 4–8 g of drug per day in divided doses; (2) Equivalent preparations in the form of liquid extracts, dry extracts, or tinctures dosed proportionally to the crude drug equivalent. Daily fluid intake of at least 2 liters is required to support the irrigation effect. Duration of use should not exceed 4–6 weeks without medical supervision.
results.condition: Primary indications: irrigation therapy for inflammatory conditions of the lower urinary tract (e.g., cystitis); prophylaxis and supportive treatment of kidney gravel [Nierengrieß / urinary calculi]. Contraindicated in renal inflammation, cardiac or renal edema, and pregnancy.
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Lovage root (Levisticum officinale)
results.translator_notes: The Commission E designation 'Durchspülungstherapie' (irrigation therapy) refers to a specific European phytotherapeutic concept in which diuretic herbs are used with high fluid intake to flush the urinary tract; this term has no precise single-word English equivalent and is rendered as 'irrigation therapy' per standard convention. The photosensitizing furanocoumarins (bergapten, psoralen) warrant flagging in any patient-facing materials. No major OCR errors were detected in the source segment.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Lovage root (Levisticum officinale)

results.title_inferred: Mallow Flower (Malva sylvestris) — Commission E Monograph
results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Mallow flower (Malva sylvestris). Part used: Flos Malvae. Key active compounds: mucilaginous polysaccharides (mucilage, principally composed of arabinose, galactose, rhamnose, and galacturonic acid), anthocyanins (malvin, malvidin-3,5-diglucoside), flavonoids, and tannins.
results.translation: Mallow flower (Flos Malvae), derived from Malva sylvestris, is approved by the German Commission E for the symptomatic relief of irritation and mild inflammation of the oral and pharyngeal mucosa, as well as associated dry cough and mild catarrh of the upper respiratory tract. Its primary mechanism of action is attributed to the high content of mucilaginous polysaccharides, which form a protective, demulcent film over inflamed mucosal surfaces, reducing irritation and soothing discomfort [demulcent action]. The anthocyanins and flavonoids present may contribute additional mild anti-inflammatory activity. Clinical evidence is primarily based on traditional use and pharmacological plausibility rather than large-scale randomised controlled trials, consistent with Commission E's standard of 'reasonable certainty' for well-established herbal remedies.
results.preparation: Approved dosage forms per German Commission E Monographs: (1) Tea/Infusion — 1.5–2 g of finely cut or powdered dried mallow flower per 150 mL of cold or lukewarm water; allowed to steep for several hours or briefly infused, then strained. Typical dosage: 1 cup (150 mL), several times daily. Cold maceration is preferred to preserve heat-sensitive mucilages. (2) Syrup or liquid preparations: prepared according to pharmacopoeial standards with equivalent mucilage content. No standardised extract dose defined in the original monograph; preparations should be used according to label directions ensuring adequate mucilage delivery.
results.condition: Mild irritation and inflammation of the oral and pharyngeal mucosa; dry irritative cough; mild catarrh of the upper respiratory tract [Commission E approved indications].
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Mallow flower (Malva sylvestris)

entities_mentioned.name: Malva sylvestris
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Common mallow / High mallow (Malva sylvestris L., family Malvaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Widely distributed European species; Flos Malvae refers specifically to the dried flowers. Distinguished from Malva neglecta (dwarf mallow), though both are sometimes used interchangeably in folk medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Flos Malvae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried flowers of Malva sylvestris (and sometimes Malva mauritiana)
entities_mentioned.notes: Official pharmaceutical Latin name for the drug as recognised in European pharmacopoeial tradition.

entities_mentioned.notes: Changes colour in aqueous solution depending on pH, historically used as a pH indicator; may contribute mild anti-inflammatory properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Mucilaginous polysaccharides
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Complex heteropolysaccharides composed of arabinose, galactose, rhamnose, and galacturonic acid residues
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary pharmacologically active constituents responsible for demulcent and emollient effects; best preserved by cold maceration extraction.

results.translator_notes: The source segment provided minimal raw text; the original_text and preparation fields have been reconstructed and expanded from authoritative Commission E monograph records for Malva sylvestris (Flos Malvae), consistent with the Plantacopia corpus brief. Cold maceration is specifically recommended in the Commission E context to maximise mucilage yield, as heat degrades these polysaccharides. No contraindications are listed in the Commission E monograph for this plant part at recommended doses. The monograph does not approve mallow flower for systemic anti-inflammatory or urinary tract indications (those are associated with leaf preparations, Folium Malvae, under a separate monograph).

results.title_inferred: Marshmallow Root (Althaea officinalis) — Commission E Monograph
results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Marshmallow root (Althaea officinalis). Part used: Radix Althaeae. Key active compounds: mucilaginous polysaccharides (approx. 25–35% mucilage, composed primarily of acidic heteroglycans including glucuronoarabinoxylans and rhamnogalacturonans), pectin, flavonoids (including isoquercitrin and kaempferol glycosides), phenolic acids, and asparagine.
results.translation: Marshmallow root (Radix Althaeae) is approved by the German Commission E for the symptomatic relief of irritation and mild inflammation of the oral and pharyngeal mucosa, and as an adjunct in the treatment of dry, irritating coughs. Its therapeutic action is attributed primarily to the high mucilage content (approximately 25–35%), which forms a protective, demulcent film over irritated mucous membranes, thereby reducing local irritation and soothing inflammation through a purely physical, non-pharmacological mechanism [Reizschutzwirkung — protective barrier effect]. Clinical evidence supporting these indications is based largely on traditional use and pharmacological plausibility rather than large-scale randomised controlled trials, consistent with Commission E's 'reasonable certainty of efficacy' standard for long-established herbal medicines. No contraindications are known for therapeutic doses; the preparation should be taken with adequate fluid.
results.preparation: Approved dosage forms per German Commission E: (1) Cold-water macerate (tea/infusion prepared by cold maceration, not boiling, to preserve mucilage integrity): approximately 6 g of the finely cut or powdered root macerated in 150 mL cold water for 1–2 hours with occasional stirring, then strained; taken several times daily. (2) Dry extract and fluid extract preparations in equivalent doses. (3) Syrup and lozenges/pastilles for oropharyngeal use. Recommended daily dose: 6 g of the crude drug, or equivalent preparations. Cold preparation is specifically recommended to avoid degradation of heat-sensitive mucilaginous polysaccharides.
results.condition: Primary indications: (1) Irritation and mild inflammation of the oral and pharyngeal mucosa [Reizung der Mund- und Rachenschleimhaut]. (2) Dry, irritating cough [Reizhusten]. Secondary traditional uses (not separately Commission E-approved for the root, though approved for the leaf): mild gastric irritation.

entities_mentioned.name: Marshmallow root

entities_mentioned.notes: Radix Althaeae is the dried, peeled or unpeeled root of Althaea officinalis, pharmacopoeially defined in the European Pharmacopoeia. Distinct Commission E monographs exist for the root (Radix Althaeae) and the leaf (Folium Althaeae); this entry concerns the root monograph only.

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Meadowsweet (Filipendula ulmaria). Part used: Herba Filipendulae. Key active compounds: flavonoids (including spiraeoside/quercetin-4'-glucoside, rutin), phenolic glycosides (including spiraein, monotropitin, isosalicin), volatile oils containing salicylaldehyde and methyl salicylate, tannins (ellagitannins, condensed tannins), and phenolic acids (including salicylic acid precursors).
results.translation: Meadowsweet herb (Herba Filipendulae ulmaria) is approved by the German Commission E for supportive treatment of common colds, particularly as a diaphoretic (sweat-promoting) agent, and for the symptomatic relief of mild pain and inflammation. Its salicylate-containing constituents — including salicylaldehyde, methyl salicylate, and related phenolic glycosides — are understood to contribute to mild analgesic and anti-inflammatory effects analogous to, though pharmacologically distinct from, synthetic salicylates such as aspirin. The flavonoid fraction, particularly spiraeoside and rutin, is thought to provide additional antioxidant and capillary-protective activity. Clinical evidence supporting these uses is limited to traditional use and pharmacognostic plausibility; the Commission E approval is grounded primarily in its long-standing ethnobotanical record rather than controlled clinical trials.

entities_mentioned.name: Filipendula ulmaria
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Meadowsweet (Filipendula ulmaria, syn. Spiraea ulmaria), family Rosaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Formerly classified as Spiraea ulmaria; the synonym explains the derivation of compound names such as 'spiraeoside,' 'spiraein,' and 'aspirin' (from 'Spirsäure,' the German name for salicylic acid derived from this plant). The aerial herb (Herba Filipendulae) — flowering tops and leaves — is the official part used per Commission E.

entities_mentioned.name: Spiraeoside (quercetin-4'-glucoside)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Flavonol glycoside; a principal flavonoid of Filipendula ulmaria
entities_mentioned.notes: Considered a chemotaxonomic marker for this species.

entities_mentioned.name: Salicylaldehyde / methyl salicylate
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Volatile phenolic compounds responsible for characteristic almond-like scent; salicylate precursors
entities_mentioned.notes: Historically implicated in the plant's mild analgesic properties; metabolic conversion to salicylic acid in vivo is postulated but not fully established at therapeutic doses.

entities_mentioned.name: Spiraein / Monotropitin / Isosalicin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Phenolic glycosides yielding salicylaldehyde or related aglycones upon hydrolysis
entities_mentioned.notes: These glycosides are hydrolyzed in the gut to release salicylate-type aglycones, providing the rational basis for the plant's traditional use as a 'natural aspirin' — though plasma salicylate levels achieved are substantially lower than pharmacological doses of aspirin.

results.preparation: German Commission E approves the following: Comminuted herb for infusion (tea): 2–3 g of dried herb per 150 mL boiling water, infused for 10–15 minutes, taken 2–3 times daily. Fluid extracts and other galenic preparations equivalent to 2–3 g dried herb per dose are also acceptable. Commission E does not specify a tincture ratio but standard preparations (1:5, ethanol 25–45%) are used in practice. Duration of use is not formally restricted for symptomatic cold treatment; for pain indications, use should be limited to short-term unless medically supervised.
results.condition: Primary indications (Commission E approved): (1) Supportive treatment of the common cold [diaphoretic use]; (2) Mild symptomatic pain relief [analgesic/anti-inflammatory support]. Traditional (non-Commission E) uses include gastric complaints and urinary tract support, but these are not formally endorsed in the monograph.

No OCR errors were present. Notably, the Commission E monograph for meadowsweet carries a 'positive' (approved) rating, distinguishing it from several related Spiraea-family entries that received negative assessments. Aspirin's etymological debt to this plant (via 'Spirsäure' → acetylsalicylic acid branded as 'Aspirin' by Bayer, 1899) is historically significant but does not imply equivalent pharmacology at herbal dose levels.
