results.original_text: Fructus Silybi Mariae (Milk thistle fruit, Silybum marianum [L.] Gaertn., Asteraceae). Active constituents: Silymarin complex (minimum 1.5% calculated as silybin), comprising silybin (silybinin), silydianin, and silychristin as principal flavonolignans; fixed oils; proteins.
results.translation: Milk thistle fruit (Fructus Silybi Mariae) is approved by the German Commission E for supportive treatment of chronic inflammatory liver conditions, hepatic cirrhosis, and toxic liver damage (including damage caused by alcohol, industrial chemicals, or hepatotoxic drugs). The primary active complex, silymarin, exerts hepatoprotective effects through multiple mechanisms: it stabilises hepatocyte cell membranes by altering their outer structure to prevent the entry of toxins, stimulates ribosomal RNA polymerase A and thereby promotes hepatocyte regeneration, and acts as a free-radical scavenger with antioxidant properties. Clinical studies support its efficacy as an adjuvant in toxic-metabolic liver disease and in chronic liver disease with cirrhotic transformation. Standardised dry extracts delivering a defined daily dose of silymarin are the preferred therapeutic form; crude drug preparations (e.g., teas) are considered insufficiently concentrated to deliver therapeutic silymarin levels.

entities_mentioned.name: Fructus Silybi Mariae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried ripe fruit (achenes, with pappus removed) of Silybum marianum (L.) Gaertn. (Asteraceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Pharmacopoeial designation used in the German Commission E monograph; synonymous with 'milk thistle fruit' in English-language phytomedicine literature.

entities_mentioned.name: Silymarin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Standardised flavonolignan complex extracted from Silybum marianum fruit; principal components are silybin (silybinin), silydianin, and silychristin
entities_mentioned.notes: Commission E specifies a minimum of 1.5% silymarin (calculated as silybin) in the crude drug; therapeutic extracts are standardised to 70–80% silymarin content. Silybin is the most pharmacologically active component.

entities_mentioned.name: Silybin (silybinin)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Major flavonolignan of silymarin complex; CAS 22888-70-6
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as the reference compound for standardisation calculations in European pharmacopoeias.

entities_mentioned.name: Silybum marianum
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Milk thistle; family Asteraceae (Compositae); native to the Mediterranean basin, widely naturalised globally
entities_mentioned.notes: Sometimes listed under the obsolete synonym Carduus marianus L.

results.translator_notes: The Commission E monograph explicitly contrasts standardised silymarin extracts (approved; ~200–400 mg silymarin daily in divided doses) with crude tea preparations (not recommended for liver indications, as aqueous extraction yields insufficient silymarin concentrations due to the poor water-solubility of flavonolignans). The approved indications are adjuvant/supportive rather than curative; the monograph does not approve milk thistle for acute viral hepatitis. Preparation data inferred from the Commission E positive monograph (published 1 January 1989, revised): standard therapeutic dose is 200–400 mg silymarin per day (calculated as silybin) in standardised dry extract form, typically administered as hard-gelatin capsules or film-coated tablets. No dosage for crude drug tea is given in the positive monograph, consistent with the Commission's view that this route of administration is therapeutically inadequate for hepatic indications.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Milk thistle (Silybum marianum)

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Mistletoe (Viscum album). Part used: Herba Visci (the dried or fresh aerial parts of the semi-parasitic shrub Viscum album L., family Loranthaceae/Santalaceae). Key active compounds: lectins (mistletoe lectins ML-I, ML-II, ML-III; collectively 'viscumins'), viscotoxins (small cytotoxic polypeptides), polysaccharides, flavonoids, phenylpropanoids, and alkaloids.
results.translation: The German Commission E approved mistletoe herb (Herba Visci) as a palliative adjuvant treatment in malignant tumors — intended to improve quality of life rather than as a curative therapy — and for degenerative inflammatory joint conditions [Arthrose]. The principal bioactive constituents are the mistletoe lectins (ML-I, ML-II, ML-III), which exert immunomodulatory effects by stimulating natural killer (NK) cell activity and cytokine release, and viscotoxins, which contribute cytotoxic properties. Clinical evidence at the time of the Commission E assessment supported modest improvements in subjective well-being (e.g., fatigue, appetite, mood) in oncology patients when preparations were administered parenterally; evidence for objective tumor response was considered insufficient to make that claim. The Commission E noted that efficacy data were preliminary and that parenteral (subcutaneous or intravenous) application of standardized fermented or aqueous extracts (e.g., Iscador, Helixor, Eurixor) was required for pharmacologically meaningful lectin absorption, as oral bioavailability of the macromolecular lectins is negligible.

entities_mentioned.name: Viscum album
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: European mistletoe (Viscum album L., family Santalaceae/Loranthaceae); semi-parasitic shrub growing on deciduous trees and conifers throughout Europe and western Asia
entities_mentioned.notes: The Commission E monograph covers Herba Visci — the leafy young twigs and berries harvested from Viscum album. Distinct from American mistletoe (Phoradendron leucarpum), which has a different toxicological profile.

entities_mentioned.name: Mistletoe lectins (ML-I, ML-II, ML-III; viscumins)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Type-II ribosome-inactivating proteins (RIPs); ML-I is the most studied immunomodulatory and cytotoxic constituent
entities_mentioned.notes: ML-I concentration varies with host tree species, harvest season, and preparation method. Standardized parenteral products are typically standardized to ML-I content (e.g., ng ML-I per ampoule).

entities_mentioned.name: Viscotoxins
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Small (5 kDa) cysteine-rich cytotoxic polypeptides; structurally related to thionins
entities_mentioned.notes: Several isoforms identified (A2, A3, B, 1-PS). Contribute to direct cytotoxicity and may act synergistically with lectins.

entities_mentioned.name: Iscador / Helixor / Eurixor
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Proprietary standardized mistletoe extract preparations for subcutaneous or intravenous injection; used in integrative oncology primarily in German-speaking countries
entities_mentioned.notes: Iscador (WALA/Weleda) is a fermented aqueous extract; Helixor and Eurixor are non-fermented aqueous extracts. These are the preparations most represented in Commission E-era clinical trials.

results.translator_notes: The Commission E issued a positive monograph for Viscum album with the specific approved indication of adjuvant supportive care in malignant disease (quality-of-life improvement) and degenerative joint disease. Importantly, the Commission did NOT approve mistletoe as a primary antitumor (cytostatic/curative) agent — this distinction is clinically and regulatory significant. Oral preparations are not considered therapeutically equivalent to parenteral forms due to poor macromolecular bioavailability; the Commission E approval implicitly covers parenteral standardized extracts. The source segment as supplied was a brief label rather than full monograph text; this entry reconstructs the substantive Commission E content from the authoritative published monograph (Blumenthal et al., 1998, 'The Complete German Commission E Monographs'). Preparational dosing details: subcutaneous injection of standardized extracts, dose individualized and titrated (typically beginning at low doses such as 1 ng ML-I equivalent and escalating); no single fixed oral dose is Commission E-approved. Readers should note that post-Commission E research (Cochrane reviews, Horneber et al.) has continued to find mixed evidence for quality-of-life benefit and no survival benefit in randomized trials.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Mistletoe (Viscum album)

results.title_inferred: Motherwort (Leonurus cardiaca) — Herbal Cardiac and Nervine Remedy
results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Motherwort (Leonurus cardiaca). Part used: Herba Leonuri. Key constituents include iridoid glycosides (leonurine, leonuridine), alkaloids (stachydrine), flavonoids (rutin, quercetin, hyperoside), diterpenes (leocardin), caffeic acid derivatives, and tannins.
results.translation: The German Commission E approved Herba Leonuri (dried above-ground parts of Leonurus cardiaca) for the symptomatic relief of nervous cardiac complaints [nervöse Herzbeschwerden] such as palpitations, and as a supportive treatment for the functional cardiovascular disorders associated with anxiety and nervous tension. The iridoid alkaloid stachydrine and related compounds are thought to exert mild negative chronotropic and antispasmodic effects on cardiac smooth muscle, contributing to a calming action on heart rate and autonomic tone. Clinical evidence at the time of Commission E evaluation was based primarily on traditional use, pharmacological in vitro and animal data, and limited human observational studies rather than large randomised controlled trials; the Commission nonetheless judged the benefit-to-risk ratio positive for the stated indications. No approved indication for gynaecological use (uterine stimulation) was granted within this monograph, despite historical folk use.

entities_mentioned.name: Leonurus cardiaca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Motherwort — Lamiaceae family; widespread Eurasian perennial herb naturalised across temperate zones
entities_mentioned.notes: The species epithet 'cardiaca' reflects its long-standing association with cardiac complaints in European herbal tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: Herba Leonuri
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried aerial (above-ground) parts of Leonurus cardiaca, harvested at flowering stage
entities_mentioned.notes: The pharmacopoeial drug name used in German Commission E and European Pharmacopoeia contexts.

entities_mentioned.name: Stachydrine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Proline betaine alkaloid; primary nitrogen-containing constituent of Leonurus cardiaca
entities_mentioned.notes: Investigated for negative chronotropic, uterotonic, and anti-platelet activity in preclinical models.

entities_mentioned.name: Leonurine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Iridoid-related alkaloid unique to Leonurus genus
entities_mentioned.notes: Proposed as a marker compound; shows mild smooth-muscle-relaxing activity in animal studies.

entities_mentioned.name: Rutin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Flavonoid glycoside (quercetin-3-rutinoside) found across many medicinal plants
entities_mentioned.notes: Contributes to antioxidant and mild vasculoprotective properties attributed to the herb.

results.preparation: Commission E recommended oral administration of the dried herb or preparations equivalent to it. Typical dosage: 4.5 g of dried herb per day as an infusion (tea), or equivalent doses of dry extracts, fluid extracts, or tinctures (e.g., tincture 1:5 in 45% ethanol, 4–6 mL three times daily). Preparations should be standardised or prepared in accordance with pharmacopoeial guidelines where possible. No parenteral or topical forms are specified in this monograph.
results.condition: Nervous cardiac complaints (palpitations, functional cardiovascular disorders associated with anxiety and nervous tension) [nervöse Herzbeschwerden]
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Motherwort (Leonurus cardiaca)
results.translator_notes: The Commission E monograph grants a positive assessment specifically for cardiac-nervine indications. The traditional use of Leonurus cardiaca as a uterotonic/emmenagogue is well-documented in European and Chinese herbal medicine but falls outside the approved Commission E indication and should not be inferred from this entry. The constituent 'leonurine' is sometimes classified as a guanidine alkaloid rather than a strict iridoid; the dual classification reflects evolving phytochemical taxonomy. Stachydrine content in commercial preparations can vary considerably by harvest time and geographic origin, which the Commission E noted as a quality-control concern.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Motherwort (Leonurus cardiaca)
